output: Madame Vice-President, the Commission gives the impression of acting like a distracted student rushing to catch up on lost time before handing in their work.
Certainly, the social package exists, but it is incomplete and can only be regarded as a step toward the Commission's consideration of social issues.

The President of the Commission failed to heed the warning given by citizens in France, the Netherlands, and Ireland, which would unfortunately have been echoed elsewhere in the event of a popular vote.
These elections are reminders to get back on track.
If Europeans want more Europe, it is to build a social project, not merely to adopt yet more technical directives.
Regulation concerning the internal market is indeed necessary, but it is by no means the essential aspect.
Program 2009, like its predecessors, will miss the point.
Citizens do not expect the Commission to limit itself to completing, let alone simplifying, legislation.
They expect the Commission to live up to the task assigned to it by the treaties—the locomotive, the think tank of the Union.
It is not enough for its president to be wise and silent, standing at attention behind the President of the Council.
We expect the Commission to act as a key player in reflecting on the nature of the societal project we need.
But what is the societal project behind this program?
Program 2009 contains no major errors, but it is not the program our fellow citizens need.
Mr. President, it will be the new College that assumes its responsibilities.
I only hope he will be less deaf to the appeals of peoples than the current one.
Mr. President, first of all, a question for the Council.

What about the high-level conference on Georgia, which had been proposed by the French presidency, and how does the current EU presidency intend to uphold Georgia's territorial integrity, given that a country recognizes two separatist regions, Abkhazia and South Ossetia?
But it's a pity that Mr. Barroso isn't here, because in his speech he was unconvincing, perhaps because he himself is not convinced by the proposals he has made to us.
I believe the financial and economic situation is far more serious than he described.
Even today, I believe those who follow the internet clearly see that the stock markets continue to collapse despite intervention efforts.

For me, it is the collapse of the current system, a completely outdated system—the system that led to bankruptcy, that caused the earth to be entirely exploited and suffocated by greenhouse gas emissions, that triggered underdevelopment in the Global South, and that has also caused underdevelopment in our cities.
Madam President, Madam Commissioner, the current financial crisis clearly shows that when the problem is global, a global response is required.
And this is especially true in the field of agriculture or in the food sector.
This is why, in particular, the World Trade Organization was established, which we support and which seeks to implement global rules.

It turns out that this does not quite work as we would like, because we are faced with the following problem: we must reconcile two things—the free trade that everyone agrees is necessary, and the protections that are equally necessary.
and protecting national economies or agriculture does not mean protectionism.
To achieve this, the path we have followed for the past sixty years, since GATT, has been an unbalanced one, based on the reduction of customs duties, with the objective of their elimination.
It happens that, technically, the reduction of customs duties
It's difficult. Proof of this is the wide array of mathematical formulas— the South Korean formula, the European formula, or the most well-known one, the Swiss formula.
It doesn't really work because a product is not unitary.
It comprises numerous elements with rules originating from very different sources, and technically reducing customs duties is not as straightforward as one might think.
We are therefore in a technical deadlock, and politically, through negotiations, we attempt to find a way out of this impasse.

Now, it so happens that, technologically, scientists have developed a new customs technology: the technology of export-importer-deductible customs duties, which generates savings for the importing state's economy.
In practice, this deductible customs duty is converted into a customs credit equal to the amount of the customs duty paid by the exporter in the importing country.
This customs credit has three characteristics: it is refundable, negotiable, and eligible for discounting.
It is refundable at the time the exporter purchases from the importer, at which point the exporter may deduct the duty it has incurred.
It is negotiable because if the exporting company that has paid 100 in customs duties has nothing to purchase from the importer, it can negotiate the customs credit on the stock market or in a bank.
It is sound and reliable, if we wish to assist developing countries, for the importer to provide a customs credit amount higher than the amount of customs duties.
With this technique, a global trade currency is created whose money supply equals the amount of existing customs duties.
For example, the European Union would provide thirteen billion euros of international currency in the commercial sector.

Mr. President, the Russo-Georgian conflict and the financial crisis provided the European Union with the opportunity to establish itself on the world stage as a political actor in its own right and of the highest order.
Thanks to your determination, Mr. President of the Council, Europe has succeeded in uniting and finding coordinated, effective, and swift responses.
Faced with crises, Europe proves that it exists and demonstrates its added value.
In the matter of immigration, for example, laissez-faire was no longer appropriate.
The adoption by the European Council of the migration and asylum pact, initiated by the French presidency, represents a significant advancement.
On the fight against climate change, Europe must set concrete goals and show the way to its global partners.

You have already spoken on this matter, Mr. President of the Council, but we wish this agreement to be as balanced as possible, as it must take into account the economic situation of our countries, which are currently very unstable.
but also pose the question of the type of development we will choose for the future.
To meet the challenges ahead, the European Union more than ever needs the Treaty of Lisbon.
We understand the concerns of our Irish friends, but the status quo is not an option.
Ireland must present us with a solution at the December European Council; many of us are demanding this.
In recent weeks, we have observed with pride that a strong, politically united Europe, steadfast in adversity, resolute in its dealings with its partners, and listened to on the international stage
We wish for this new European mindset to become the norm and to endure.
The Treaty of Lisbon is the best solution we have at our disposal to achieve this.

Madam President, Minister, I was in Tbilisi when President Sarkozy came to negotiate the peace plan, and of course I pay tribute to him for acting swiftly and successfully bringing this war to an end.
But this war is, in a way, our failure—we remained hesitant for fourteen years, silently witnessing the escalation of provocations in the separatist regions.

This war has indeed awakened Europe, placing it before its responsibilities. But the flames continue to burn in the Caucasus, and we must do everything possible to finally resolve the frozen conflicts for the security of all Europe.
I am also aware, Mr. Minister, of the divisions among European countries regarding Georgia's entry into NATO, and personally, I am opposed to it.
I will make a proposal; I will ask the European Union to propose the status of neutrality for these Caucasus countries.
Only the status of neutrality will be able to ease tensions with Russia and definitively prevent this subregion from new conflicts.
This status will ensure the security of these young democracies and contribute to our own security.
Mr. Commissioner, Mr. Minister, over the past three days, the European Days of Development have taken place, during which the severe food crisis affecting the poorest countries was widely discussed.
All interventions emphasized the absolute necessity for developing countries to develop their own agriculture by optimally utilizing their land and natural resources.

In passing, the lyrical outbursts of those who called yesterday and still call today for reducing the state's intervention, public spending, unreserved liberalization, privatization, and the relegation of agriculture as
a quasi prehistoric activity, this sudden reversal in favor of massive public investments in agricultural production and food sovereignty was simultaneously somewhat absurd and scandalous.
The future of agriculture in developing countries is directly linked to the debate we are having today.

I regret to say that this assessment of the CAP's health, initiated before the peak of the food riots and the disastrous market upheavals, fails to account for the situation of southern peasant communities and does not draw all the necessary conclusions from the severe crises we are currently experiencing—ecological, social, economic, and financial.

I therefore deeply regret that the strong relationship between Northern and Southern agriculture is so little addressed, both in the Commission's proposals and in the Parliament's report. The undermining of the promised one billion euros demonstrates just how far we still have to go.
Mr. President, the report we are about to vote on tomorrow marks the culmination of a year of work and negotiations devoted to the future of the common agricultural policy and of our farmers.
Indeed, it was last November 20th that Mrs. Fischer-Boel came to present her proposals on the health check-up.
Following Lutz Goebbels' interim report, here comes the report by Capula Santos on the Commission's proposal.
We should congratulate our two colleagues for their excellent work.
The European Parliament's Agriculture Committee has managed to remain firm in the face of the Commission's initial proposals, which we considered far too liberal.
I will remain vigilant regarding the milk sector and, in particular, the measures preceding the abolition of quotas.

I am particularly thinking of mountain dairy producers, who must not become the neglected beneficiaries of this reform; we must ensure they are not overlooked, as they will require even greater support than others.
I don't wish to play the Cassandra, but how can one imagine the French dairy industry concentrated within a single region?
I therefore welcome with goodwill the proposal to establish a milk fund, as well as the one calling for assessing, as of 2010, the increase in milk quotas according to market developments.

The calculation of the progressive modulation carried out by the Agriculture Commission seems appropriate to me and would enable us to strengthen the development of territories, rural areas, and to sustainably preserve biodiversity.
I also commend the clarification of eco-conditionality, which had previously posed a genuine challenge for farmers to implement.

I also wish for us to maintain the amount of the tobacco fund, which enables us to preserve our small farms across Europe, as well as a non-negligible workforce in a region where nothing else is viable, and which helps us avoid having to import products.
Finally, Mr. Minister, we greatly appreciated your commitment throughout these negotiations.
You have shown great patience and great determination, and you have not spared your time in vain.
It was a genuine pleasure to work alongside you.
You have been extremely effective, with us.
I know that we can rely on
Mr. President, thank you first of all for the work completed and the conclusions and recommendations proposed in the health check assessment of the CAP contained in Mr. Capoulas Santos' report.
Agriculture is indeed an economic sector that cannot respond to mere market signals.
Its activity inevitably has impacts on food self-sufficiency, but also, all too often overlooked, on territories and employment.
The removal of market instruments would be a serious blow to our agriculture

Decoupling, for example, poses a genuine challenge for certain livestock regions, particularly those specializing in dairy cattle and sheep, and I welcome having heard Madame Fischer-Boll address this issue earlier today.
Moreover, it is clear that strictly maintaining historical references would severely hinder any possibility of reform.
Nevertheless, I still believe that the issue of the second pillar of the CAP is essential, because rural areas need to rely on agricultural activity to develop employment in rural regions.
And thus one must consider this issue of the second foot without, of course, amputating the first.
Given that Minister Jouillet is about to leave us, I believe this Parliament should thank him.
He was one of the most diligent ministers we have ever known.
I wish him good luck.

Madam President, I voted in favor of the PERI report on the integration of the OCM twenty into the single OCM, though with deep regret, because I believe this single OCM is not a simplification and does not enhance transparency.
It will make life more complicated for viticulturists and the entire wine industry.
Last night, the commissioner tried to reassure us

I hope the Commission will keep its promise, and above all that the profession will continue to be adequately represented on the advisory committee, as has been the case since the first Common Organization of the Market twenty years ago.
Sir.
Mr. President, Mr. Minister, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues, our group enthusiastically supports the Small Business Act proposal that the Commission presented to us last June.

Indeed, since the year 2000, initiatives have been taken in favor of SMEs, and I particularly have in mind the excellent work of our colleague Dominique Blasto on the SME charter—initiatives that were appreciated but considered insufficient, as they often remained little more than pious wishes.
Today, we must go further.
The serious economic crisis our countries are experiencing, whose consequences are particularly concerning for SMEs, invites us to do so, if further encouragement were needed.
In this regard, the Small Business Act embodies a new, resolutely proactive approach.
In a few weeks, we will examine Mrs. Herzog's report on the Commission's communication, but today, through the submission of this oral question, we wished to address the Council.

Mr. Minister, we have noted with interest the conclusions adopted yesterday by the Competitiveness Council, and we would like to know whether the upcoming European Council on the 11th and 12th of December

will clearly reiterate its conclusions and provide strong impetus to ensure that the overarching guiding principle of "Think small first" is swiftly incorporated into the regulatory frameworks of the Member States.
We wish for the Council to commit to the principle of the attached action plan, accompanied by concrete measures.
In this regard, and within the limited time available to me, I will emphasize three directions in which we need binding impulses.
impact assessments, which should be systematic, and regular monitoring, with oversight by the European Parliament, of the implementation status of the Small Business Act in the Member States,
access to public procurement,

The code of conduct has no binding effect; we must together be more imaginative and innovative, and propose concrete, appropriate measures that genuinely open up these markets to small and medium-sized enterprises.
Finally, access to financing, more indispensable than ever in the current context.
Your proposals are good, Mr. Minister; they must also include support for supply.
Urgency requires immediate action.
Please confirm to us the Council's intention.

Mr. President, Mr. Minister, Mr. Commissioner, we all know that small and medium-sized enterprises are the backbone of the European Union, since they employ more than seventy percent of the Union's workforce and account for ninety-nine percent of European enterprises.

But as already mentioned, these millions of small and medium-sized enterprises are now distressed and threatened by the financial crisis, as it erodes household purchasing power and confidence, and affects order backlogs.

It is therefore the cash flow of SMEs that is at risk, and consequently, measures must be taken to support their operations by ensuring that banks guarantee effective access to the financing necessary for the continuity of their activities.

This is precisely the central issue addressed in the conclusions of the Competitiveness Council of December 1st, which the Minister has mentioned, and in the recovery plan approved by the European Ministers of Finance.
Making the needs of SMEs a permanent political priority for the Union is essential, and I wish to welcome the action plan in support of the Small Business Act for Europe.
It indeed reflects the intention to ensure the effective implementation of the guiding principle of giving priority to small ones first, that is, to European SMEs.
However, as the Commissioner has said, and as we all maintain, the Council must genuinely engage with the other institutions
European. For this principle to now become effective, it is necessary to go beyond the declaration and move to implementation across all European policies and programs.
However, we can say that this action plan represents a genuine response to the urgency of the situation.
And, Mr. Minister, I would like to commend the determination of the French presidency of the Union, which enabled intentions to be swiftly translated into action.

Madam President in office, Commissioner, I fully support the rapporteur, who is upholding the position that was ours during the first reading, a position rejected by the governments of the Member States.
It was urgent to bring our legislation on rest periods into line with the case law of the Court of Justice, and this has now been done.
The Cercas report provides balanced and employee-protective responses.
All time spent on duty is counted as working time.
The compensatory rest takes place immediately after the service period.
It is a matter of common sense to ensure decent working conditions, particularly for medical professions.
But the reform of the working time directive also provides us with an opportunity to advance our European social law by abolishing the individual opt-out.
The Cercas report seizes this opportunity and proposes a progressive elimination of any possibility to derogate from the maximum legal working time.
One must face reality squarely.
It is a joke to claim that the employee is in an equal position vis-à-vis their employer and can refuse what is offered.
Dear colleagues, we must clearly show to the government of the Member States that the text it wishes to impose upon us is unacceptable.

In the chorus of praise that will undoubtedly soon soothe the ears of the French government, it seems essential for us to focus first on the employees who will be compelled to work ever more, without truly having a choice, and likewise on all those who will be asked tomorrow to work on Sundays in France.
Indeed, it is clearly in order to open up this possibility that the French government has changed its position on individual opt-out at the Council.

Dear colleagues, let us listen to the workers and strive to answer their call, lest the negative votes in the French, Dutch, and Irish referendums be followed by many others, undermining an European Union that leaves them feeling its policies do not address their everyday concerns. Thank you.
Mr. President, this was a very successful French Presidency.
There were very few dissenting voices in not agreeing.
The twenty-seven set aside their differences to reach a series of decisions that were exemplary.
On the climate-energy package, Europe is taking its first step, and in this regard, that first step is immense.
She now has the means to assume her environmental responsibility without sacrificing her economy.
She can now take the lead in this matter, guiding the other continents toward sustainable development.
Moreover, Ireland proposes a scenario for overcoming its isolation.
Our Irish friends will once again vote on the Lisbon Treaty, armed with all the information, since practically all the other members will have already made their final decisions.
Current crises demonstrate that the Union more than ever needs the best institutional tools to be effective.
The European Council adopted a large-scale, coordinated economic recovery plan, based on long-term productive investment and targeted support for the industrial sectors most severely affected.
President Sarkozy raised the issue of reducing certain VAT rates, and I fully share his viewpoint.

Let us hope that the ministers of economics and finance will succeed in reaching an agreement in March, an outcome that seemed impossible to the heads of state and government during the previous weekend.
The challenges facing Europeans provide the Union with the opportunity to reveal itself to them as more united, more responsive, and more committed to protecting them.
We are grateful to the French Presidency for having injected this new momentum.
We wish, like Nicolas Sarkozy, never again to have to endure the kinds of meetings of yesterday that dragged on until four in the morning just to negotiate a few trivialities.
Over the past six months, Europe has indeed undergone a change in mindset, scope, and credibility.
We must thank the French Presidency, and we are fully aware that it is by no means entirely unrelated. Thank you, Mr. President.
The situation in Gaza is known to everyone.
What we are discussing here in Parliament is natural.

But we are calling on the Security Council to take a stance, we are calling on the relevant bodies to take a stance, we are calling on the European Union to take a stance. And we, in Parliament, are debating and do not wish to take a stance.

I believe that, despite everything, given the urgency of the situation in Gaza, it is of utmost importance that this Parliament speaks out and clearly states exactly what it wants in order to put an end to the massacres currently taking place in the Middle East.
I find unacceptable that this Parliament lacks the courage and clarity to adopt a resolution following our debate.
That is why our group requests that the decision of the Presidents' Conference be revised and that we conclude the debate we must have
to take a stance on the situation in Gaza through a resolution that will clearly and firmly demonstrate this Parliament's majority position, so that the massacres in Gaza can stop.

We want a resolution; we must be aware that we bear a political responsibility regarding what is happening, and this political responsibility cannot merely be a discussion—it must be a resolution that clearly states what we want and what we condemn.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, more than ever, the proposed directive which the Parliament is about to vote on evokes in me a very mixed feeling.
It is true that the rapporteur, Mr. Klins, has carried out extensive work over many years and has negotiated skillfully, particularly during the trilogue meetings.

It is also true that the directive represents a number of significant advancements towards better functioning of the internal market and the collective management of financial assets, and is welcomed as such by the European investment fund industry.

It remains true that debates and discussions within the Council and the Parliament were never entirely free, since for certain actors the outcomes were predetermined; it was imperative, at all costs, to introduce the European passport for management companies.
On principle, there is nothing to object to—the passport holds up quite well. However, one must ensure that the introduction of the passport for management companies
It includes necessary safeguards, particularly regarding fund supervision, as it leads to a separation of functions across borders.
Moreover, I regret to note that the system put in place is not only complex, but also subject to divergent interpretations.
It may quickly encounter practical difficulties that will harm the European industry, particularly its exports to third countries.
I have duly noted that my position is in the minority; nevertheless, I do not feel uncomfortable.
Under normal circumstances, I would have been tempted to say that only practice will allow us to draw definitive conclusions on this matter and thus determine
Who is right between those who trust the market participants' ability to adapt and those who adhere to a more cautious approach?
It is not impossible that success lies ahead, but on the other hand, nothing guarantees it either.
Moreover, the very recent news, particularly the Madoff scandal and its implications for the collective asset management industry, is not reassuring.
Skepticism gives way to my unease.
The investment fund industry will not be spared by the financial crisis; we are already witnessing this.
Fundamental challenges may be necessary, within this context of crisis
That, in this context of profound crisis, we make choices that dilute the responsibilities of actors or assume perfect cooperation among regulatory authorities may indeed appear incongruous.
I am telling you as I truly believe: this directive reflects the mindset of a bygone era, prior to the crisis.
It is marked by a certain carelessness.
Indeed, the financial system is about to undergo a deep and lasting crisis of confidence, and entire sections of its architecture must be rethought.

Let us not forget, the primary function of UCITS is to protect the investor through risk diversification and the establishment of strict rules. By voting on this text in its current form, without truly knowing where we are headed, we are acting as though nothing—nothing at all—had happened.
This autism portends nothing good; that is why I shall abstain, even though I have signed the compromise amendments negotiated with the Council.
Given the circumstances, the improvements made seem indeed of little weight in comparison with the principle issues I have just explained.

Mr. President, although the regulation on animal reporting has been in effect since January 2007, it appears that Member States are not systematically complying by submitting the annual reports required of them.
Moreover, a comprehensive analysis remains so arbitrary since many elements regarding the measures implemented to carry out inspections are missing.
As a member of animal protection associations and a member of the parliamentary group for animal welfare, this issue concerns me in particular.
Associations have been desperately fighting for decades to establish guidelines in this area, where Member States have shown a certain laxity, only achieving this in 2007.
I would even go so far as to speak of a certain unwillingness, because after all, we know that implementing controls and inspections is by no means insurmountable.
We know the slaughterhouses, we know the breeding sites, we know the livestock transit routes. So, where is the problem?

I believe it is important, in our time when public opinion is increasingly, and rightly, sensitive to animal welfare—whether in terms of breeding, slaughter, or transport conditions—that states respect these sentiments.

Since I have the floor, I would like to add, with regard to transportation, that it would be essential to examine and take into account, independently of transportation durations, the local climatic conditions.
Indeed, a several-hour journey undertaken in the Netherlands during spring bears no resemblance to the same duration of travel during peak summer in a country such as Greece.
Shouldn't nighttime transportation be imposed in these final cases?
I thank you for considering this proposal in the future.

I am pleased that it is a Briton, our colleague Parish, who has submitted this text, asserting that Roman soldiers two thousand years ago were indeed right to go to Great Britain and bring amphorae of olive oil and wine.
But then, two conclusions must be drawn.

It is not very reasonable, symbolically, to locate the food agency in Helsinki, and it is unreasonable for the European Commission not to reconsider the following points: first, restore aid for almond trees; second, halt the

Stop the attacks being carried out against olive trees, particularly in Andalusia and throughout Europe; stop the attacks being made against Mediterranean bluefin tuna fishermen; and halt the attacks against the European vineyards through uprooting.
I suggest our Commissioner, our President, Mr. Barroso, organize a grand banquet in Portugal featuring sheep's cheese and wine from
We have all indeed campaigned for the closure of Guantanamo, and we all welcome President Obama's decision to shut down this prison of shame.
Today, the question of accommodating Guantanamo detainees should not arise for us.
As Europeans committed to our values of defending human rights, we must assume our responsibilities, for it is our duty to welcome these former detainees.
And one could read—here I am addressing the Council—that the press reported some member states were not very enthusiastic about this reception.
I would like to issue a sincere appeal to the Council, and in particular to the hesitant Member States, to accept hosting these detainees on their territory.

And I would say to you, dear colleagues, that the worst outcome would be if the positive momentum generated by the closure of Guantanamo were to stall simply because we had failed to respond when Europe was called upon.

This is important because the European animal feed sector constitutes one of the most significant agricultural sectors, both in terms of production—representing half of the agricultural output in the European Union, approximately 120 million tonnes—and turnover—around 50 billion euros.
The European Union indeed has five million farmers and sixty million households owning pets.

In the past, the European Union has had to manage a number of health crises, which means that today we must be more vigilant regarding transparency in order to meet the expectations of farmers as well as those of consumers.

The provisions outlined in this report have the advantage of imposing minimal costs on industries while delivering substantial benefits to consumers, who are increasingly concerned about the quality of the products they purchase.

Adopting this report will help mitigate risks by ensuring improved product quality, better control, enhanced traceability, and greater information availability for the farmer, and ultimately for the consumer.
Today, with the growing volume of international exchanges, it is more than imperative to strengthen every preventive measure so that the food crises we have experienced in the past do not recur.

This report succeeds in reconciling the right to information, while strictly defining the nutritional elements that must necessarily appear on labels, with the right to intellectual property, which is so important for preserving the competitiveness of our industries.

I believe that manufacturers should indeed be required to immediately notify the use of any new raw material entering into the composition of animal feed, in order to ensure transparency and facilitate oversight by the competent authorities.
The inclusion of an emergency procedure enabling the addition of a new hazardous substance to the list of prohibited substances seems entirely indispensable to me.

Similarly, enabling the producer to challenge the competent national authority or the European Commission in case of doubt regarding an unfair claim further strengthens the system, thereby better protecting the customer while maintaining the integrity of commercial transactions.

I therefore wish to express my full support for this excellent report, as experience shows that regulations on animal feed labelling— I conclude, Mr. President—prioritising quality, transparency, traceability and control, are the best guarantee for preventing future health crises in Europe.

Mr. President, Ladies and Gentlemen, it is certainly not Europe, nor its High Representative for Foreign Affairs, Javier Solana, former Secretary General of NATO, who can play the role of mediator between Israel and the Palestinians.
At best, are they expected to finance the reconstruction of the Gaza Strip, as they currently do for Kosovo, Lebanon, or Afghanistan?
Americans and Israelis bomb, Europeans fund reconstruction.
This is the division of tasks among the allies.
Otherwise, it would have to be the vandals who pay.
Egypt is at the center of peace negotiations concerning a prolonged ceasefire with Hamas.

But the challenge ahead is formidable, given that the new Israeli government, under pressure from the third party—Liberman, leader of an extreme right-wing faction accepted there democratically—may find its task particularly complicated in these negotiations.
Indeed, Netanyahu, who is presented as the future Prime Minister, has consistently opposed a ceasefire with Hamas.
Another difficulty is that Mahmoud Abbas's Palestinian Authority has become in the West Bank an international protectorate whose legitimacy has significantly declined among the population.
The last factor to consider is the ongoing expansion of Israeli settlements, which has never ceased since 1967, making the establishment of a Palestinian state in the West Bank particularly delicate.
Today, the ball is in Israel's court, but do the hawks on both sides truly accept this divine ceasefire that both parties demand without taking the necessary steps to achieve it?
Allow me to add a reflection on France's reintegration into the integrated military structure of NATO, which will soon be discussed in the context of the Vatanen report.
This readmission will impose strict obligations on France.
Indeed, we are rejoining NATO at a time when the Cold War has been over for ninety years.
Sarkozy appears to have forgotten the fall of the Berlin Wall and Russia's return to the ranks of free nations.
Should the logic of blocs be reinforced at a time when multipolarity is rising, along with the growing influence of emerging countries, including militarily?
Moreover, France's membership in the integrated structure will oblige it to strengthen its contingent in Afghanistan, even though it already has three personnel on the ground.
With what budget will she then finance it, given that her defense budget will fall below 2% of GDP and that more than thirty regiments will be abolished?
Paradoxically, we will increase our budgetary contribution to join NATO while simultaneously reducing our military presence in Africa.
The European defense dear to President Sarkozy will therefore be a cornerstone of the Atlantic alliance.
One needed only to read the Treaty of Lisbon and its additional protocols to be convinced.
Whether in the realm of foreign policy or common security, the European path is a dead end that will lead only to alignment with the Americans and their allies.
It is this logic of erasure that we reject in the name of sovereignty and national independence, particularly grounded in our own autonomous nuclear deterrent.
Madam President, I congratulate our three rapporteurs for this rather remarkable synthesis they have produced of the current state of our common foreign and security policy.

Mr. High Representative, you know better than anyone else that Europe is called upon today on the world stage to assume the position to which its economic and demographic strength, together with the richness of its democratic and humanist values, entitles it.

It must be acknowledged that despite the consistent willingness expressed by more than two-thirds of our European fellow citizens, as you have stated, certain progress has indeed been made over the past ten years, which I concede; yet it must also be acknowledged that this Europe still does not exist.
If further recent illustration were needed, I would be here to demonstrate it in the solution to the new crisis in the Middle East.
There has always been, and continues to be, a need for a European presence in Gaza to play an active role, both in supporting the survival of the population and in reconstruction.
and in the fight against arms smuggling that had enabled this territory to be transformed into a launch base for rockets of all calibers.
Despite the favorable statements made to Charmeltier and Jerusalem, nothing has yet been done in this regard.

I am raising here again a question previously posed during the Lebanese crisis: when, Mr. High Representative, can we expect the deployment of a European fleet in the Mediterranean, as we did in response to piracy?
We have the means to do so.
Will we ever have the will for it?
Well, Mr. Commissioner, Mr. President, my esteemed colleagues, the Parliament's decision to accept the urgency requested by the Council for the
The handling of this file has been unanimous, both last week at the Fisheries Committee and this morning, here in plenary session.
We are all indeed aware of the need for the European Union to honor the commitments made in Marrakech last November by the competent international body, the CICTA.
It specifically involves implementing the recommendations adopted by this organization in order to address the ongoing problem of overfishing of bluefin tuna in the
in the Eastern Atlantic and in the Mediterranean, and to address the serious risks arising from them for the future of the species and thus for the fishery itself.
Two of our colleagues from the Fisheries Committee, Ms. Fraga and Mr. Romeva i Rueda, were present in Marrakech and will speak about it during this debate.
In my view, I would like to emphasize the importance our committee attaches to this file, which involves the key dimensions of the common fisheries policy, namely management.
the resource, fleet management, compliance with international, regional, and bilateral agreements, technical measures, and above all, control—I will return to this shortly.
This characteristic file consequently serves, in our view, as a test of the credibility of this common fisheries policy.
For this reason, it was obviously out of the question that the implementation of the new recovery plan take place without consulting the European Parliament.
I therefore welcome the Commission's eventual choice of the only legally and politically acceptable path for implementing the directive.
recommendations of the CCTV, namely that of a properly drafted regulation proposal, based on Article 37 of the Treaty.

The transposition into Community law of commitments undertaken by the Commission on behalf of the European Union within regional fisheries organizations is by no means a trivial exercise, and we emphasize that it must always be carried out under the democratic oversight of our institution.
Substantively, I welcome the various measures proposed, which, although binding for our operators, are commensurate with the challenges at hand, and I emphasize that the most significant ones
The most important of these measures are undoubtedly those relating to monitoring, as it is true that without monitoring, no restoration plan can be effective.
Therefore, Mr. Commissioner, I thank you for this proposal and invite you to show the same
determination in implementing the plan concretely, once it has been approved by the Council, which you have demonstrated in reaching a satisfactory agreement at the level of the CICTA.
Madam President, Madam Commissioner, I regret that the Council and the Commission have not yet taken concrete steps to define territorial cohesion.
The French presidency attempted it, and I wish to commend this initiative.
Our Parliament continues to insist that this objective be applicable as of the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, and that its scope be clarified as soon as possible.

I wish, like the excellent report by Mr. Van Nistelrooy, that territorial cohesion become the legal basis ensuring the balanced development of all territories of the Union and enabling us to fully capitalize on the specific characteristics of each European region.
It is essential to better coordinate community policies in order to maximize the impact of on-the-ground interventions.
Territorial cohesion is not intended to target exclusively regions suffering from permanent disadvantages.
It must nevertheless focus on the polycentric development of the European Union as a whole, taking into account the specific characteristics of each territory while preserving their diversity.
This new concept appears relevant for addressing the ultra-peripheral regions in order to ensure their sustainable and balanced growth.

In this regard, I thank the rapporteur for having incorporated into his compromise amendments my requests concerning the specific challenges faced by the outermost regions in terms of accessibility and competitiveness—essential elements for territorial cohesion.
Upon reading the Commission's recent communication entitled "RUPs: An Asset for Europe," it appears to me that it seeks to apply to the ultra-periphery the entire
recommendations regarding the improvement of governance in cohesion matters, thus making the RUP the first, somewhat anticipatory, illustration of the implementation of territorial cohesion.

I wish that the Overseas General Assembly, which will soon begin in France, proceeds in the same manner and extensively incorporates the significant territorial impact of European policies supporting the Outermost Regions, whose added value is undeniable.
and undisputed.
One enjoys reading the conclusions of the presidency of the last Council.
It resembles the victory statement issued by every army in retreat.

They have the triviality of it, even in the repeated assertion that, once the crisis ends, the EU will build a stronger economy for the future, or in the reaffirmation of a Lisbon Strategy in full retreat.
Thus, as they lock themselves away in their glass tower, eurocrats, europhiles, and all manner of euroenthusiasts ultimately lose their grasp on reality.
Everyone knows well that the euro area was the first to enter recession, following eight years of having the world's lowest deficit rate.
But it is also the one facing the greatest difficulties in terms of industrial competitiveness and employment conditions.
Above all, it is she who, by the general disarmament of natural defenses removed from nations, is the least armed to respond.
In reality, the only merit of this crisis will be to awaken peoples lulled by the comforts of financial foresight and by anti-national propaganda.
Only states will possess the legitimate instruments necessary for any large-scale action, and will therefore have to challenge the constraints imposed by the EU and the euro.

For the French, at any rate, it is becoming increasingly clear that there will be no salvation without France, no France without French politics, and no French politics without national and popular sovereignty.

Madam President, I believe that the issue of distributing food supplies to the most disadvantaged is indeed timely; however, I think that, fundamentally, the Commission's proposal does not address the actual problem.
The issue of co-financing concerns me, and I believe it is rather the liberalism that has guided the evaluation of the CAP in recent years that should be called into question.
Food riots and abrupt fluctuations in food prices are permanent indicators of the limits of agricultural market liberalization.
This liberalization has always occurred at the expense of vulnerable populations, vulnerable producers, and fragile territories.

I therefore advocate that politics reclaim its rightful role, regain control, both at the European and global levels, and that the strategic domain of agriculture and human food, from both quantitative and qualitative perspectives, should not be subject to the rigid laws of the market.
Certainly, there is urgency, and the Sikerski report responds to this urgency, for which I commend it; however, I wish that within the context of the reflection now opening on

As for the future of the CAP, it should be recalled that the primary objective remains firmly to combat hunger and poverty structurally, as we experience them in all member states and across the world.

Rural areas are capable of meeting food needs through sufficient and high-quality production; however, they must be provided with the necessary financial and human resources to fulfill this significant responsibility.
Mr. President, during this period of economic recession, it was important that the European Union continue to demonstrate its solidarity with developing countries.
Let us recall that in 2007, the European Community and the Member States together contributed 60% of total development aid, making the European Union the world's leading donor.
European development funds enable the fight against poverty while promoting economic development and democracy.
I therefore welcome the allocation of twenty-three billion euros for ACP countries for the period 2008–2013 under the tenth EDF, which amounts to almost double the sum provided for the ninth EDF.
The Court of Auditors, in its report, concludes that the accounts of the 7th, 8th, and 9th FEDs are generally accurate, lawful, and regular.
One can only welcome the record levels of contract implementation and payment disbursement for the FED.
However, I also observe that further progress must be made to strengthen the control and monitoring system, as a significant level of errors continues to affect certain transactions.
Likewise, there is too often an excessive risk regarding budgetary support, and in my opinion, these risks should be better assessed.
On this matter, I share the view that budgetary support should only be provided if the recipient country has the capacity to manage the funds in a transparent, responsible, and effective manner.

Among the priorities we must address in the future, I would like to highlight the integration of the EDF into the general budget of the Community, as this would enhance the effectiveness and transparency of development assistance.

To conclude, Mr. President, I would like to thank the rapporteur for his excellent report, the representatives of the Court, as well as all the staff of the Commission who have carried out outstanding work on the ground. I naturally regret the absence of the Council, and in particular that of the Czech Presidency.
Madam President, the Parliament and its rapporteur were compelled to reject a poor compromise, and they were right to do so.
Our delegation has remained faithful to the position widely adopted during the plenary session, which called for the eventual elimination of all exceptions to the legal working week.
But the truth is that Parliament was the only one wanting to move things forward.
The representatives of the European people had ruled far beyond partisan divisions to put an end to this archaism known as the opt-out.
We were the only ones to propose a genuine compromise text, which the Council of Ministers superbly ignored, as did the Commission, incidentally.
The Council and the Commission have aligned themselves with those who firmly oppose any progress in workers' rights in Europe.
Mr. President of the Council, it is clear that you have achieved your objectives.
The Court of Justice requires you to regard all detention time as working time, so you have lost nothing in this respect.
The opt-out you desire still exists in practice, so the status quo has prevailed.
More than ever, the deputies elected in June will have to fight to move toward harmonization of social standards from above.
Sir.

Mr. President, this is certainly not in the name of the Libyan Commission, but rather likely in the name of my group. I would like to take this brief moment to express my exceptionally positive assessment of Mrs. Pagano's report.
Congratulations, Madam, on your report.

I believe that, in drafting this report, you have developed an extraordinarily precise set of requirements for elements that should be prominently included, Mr. Commissioner, in the Stockholm Programme, which I know the Commission is actively preparing.

Allow me to add to everything that has been said regarding the importance of judicial training, the importance of evaluation, and to emphasize two fundamental elements concerning mutual trust, which lie at the foundation of what could become mutual recognition in the future.
one, the independence of the judiciary.
At present, there are several countries within the Union where the independence of the judiciary from political power or from other forms of power has not yet been established.
It is a scandal, and this scandal must end.
Two, procedural safeguards.

As long as we cannot be certain that in a certain number of countries, individuals suspected or accused of having committed certain types of offences enjoy procedural safeguards similar to those existing in other countries, it is difficult to achieve broad acceptance of the principle of mutual recognition.
It is a fundamental aspect, and I wanted to introduce it into this debate.
Congratulations, Ms. Pagano.
Madam President, we cannot remain silent about the situation in Madagascar, and we will not be alone in condemning it.

The African Union, the Southern African Development Community, the International Organisation of La Francophonie, the Inter-Parliamentary Union, the European Union, speaking on behalf of the Commission, the

The United States, a large number of countries, including my own and Norway, to mention countries from the European continent, have condemned the coup d'état, because one must call a spade a spade—it took place in Madagascar.
We cannot remain silent, and we call for a return to constitutional legality.
We never demand nothing more than a return to square one, with, if necessary, arbitration by the Malagasy people through a consultation in the form of a presidential election or a referendum.
It is up to Madagascar's assemblies and political leaders to determine the most effective form of consultation.

What we are requesting through this joint resolution is to add our voice to that of the international community in clearly signaling to those who have seized power in a manner entirely unconstitutional.

democratic manner and in the form of a coup d'état, however well-disguised, to return to Madagascar's constitutional legality, which is one of the guarantees for the country's future development on this large island in the Indian Ocean.
Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, of course, I would like to thank Commissioner Dimas for his presence and his responses, for the attention he continues to give to all these disasters.
We worked closely together when President Barroso asked me to prepare a report, which he subsequently cited on the creation of a European civil protection force.
This report, I presented on May 9, 2006, and
When I reread it and see all the disasters that have occurred, I realize it remains ever relevant.
Madame Zavella, of course, there is more than just Greece.
All countries are affected. I would add that there are not only natural disasters, but also human-made disasters, caused by human actions.
I think of maritime disasters, the Erika and so many others, of industrial accidents such as Chernobyl.
There are other natural disasters besides fires.
I am thinking of the tsunami.
We may one day fear a tsunami in the Mediterranean, as occurred at the beginning of the 20th century, which completely destroyed the city of Messina.
Frankly, Commissioner, my dear colleagues, the tools we are mobilizing are not adequate for these disasters, which will increase under the dual impact of climate change and transportation.
And that is why I believe we must aim higher.
I thank the Commission, Commissioner Dimas, all their teams, and the Directorate-General for operating the Civil Protection mechanism.
I believe we need to go further.
Created with the member countries that wish to participate, a strengthened cooperation can be envisioned.
If we cannot muster twenty-seven to form this civil protection unit, let us begin with twelve or fifteen.
And then you will see that it will concern everyone.
When I make a list of disasters, I can say that everyone is affected—floods that hit Germany in 2002, major pandemics, and terrorism.
Here you are, Commissioner, the reason why I would wish the Commission to take the initiative to go further, and to propose to the Member States that they go further as well.
Our Parliament, the Environment Committee, and the Defense and Security Committee will support you in these proactive initiatives.

Mr. President, Commissioner, Ladies and Gentlemen Members of Parliament, once again this summer, the Mediterranean forest has been ravaged by flames in Marseille, in southern Corsica, as well as in Italy, Spain, and on a larger scale, in Greece.
More than four hundred hectares of forests and fields went up in flames, triggering substantial material losses, irreversible damage to biodiversity, and dramatic loss of human lives.
Disturbed by this recurring annual assessment and aware of the multiplying effect of climate change on wildfires,
I call for a European awareness to improve the coordination of efforts and the pooling of resources for firefighting.
I wish for the realization of a large-scale project for Euro-Mediterranean cooperation in fighting forest fires within the framework of the Union for the Mediterranean.
I welcome existing initiatives, notably the European Forest Fire Information System, a pilot project,
I nevertheless call for a reform of the European Union Solidarity Fund so that it becomes an effective tool for responding to crises and a strengthening of European resilience.
protection and the Euro-Aid mechanism envisioned by Michel Barnier as of 2006, designed to operate across the territory of the twenty-seven member countries as well as abroad.

I believe one of the keys to addressing the problem of forest fires lies in the exchange of knowledge and best practices in prevention, and I would like to highlight the pioneering role played by the forest firefighter personnel in my own department.
and I can obviously make available to Europe this example, which can be of use.
Madam President, Madam Commissioner, I will be brief.
I believe there are two elements in this debate.
The contingent question: how to get out of this crisis?
All measures that simultaneously assist farmers and involve intervention are necessary and absolutely necessary.
They must be supported, and even considered more important.
This is what we are proposing, together with a number of colleagues here in Parliament.
Secondly, there is a structurally posed question regarding the management of the milk market, and it is precisely here that we differ, Madam Commissioner.
You suggest moving toward contractualization.
I tell you in advance, contracting between industry and farmers on a forward basis, with industries competing against each other, will lead to competition on a European scale.
We need, to regulate a market, a public regulation.
There is no other alternative.
It is there that the debate will have to take place after this crisis.
I believe that on this issue, we must respect all opinions and all options, and I fear we moved too quickly at the time of the health check to resolve a matter such as quotas.

Thus far, I say this to all those who have spoken: this policy has simultaneously maintained dairy production in Europe, ensured an extremely developed dairy industry, and, at the same time, provided consumers with prices that have so far been entirely affordable. Thank you, Madam Commissioner.
Mr. President, a few days after the positive referendum result in Ireland, I truly feel that we can say today that we are more European than ever before.
I truly and serenely look forward to it.
Unfortunately, we remain essentially dependent on the decision of the Constitutional Court, as well as on the goodwill of the Czech president.

We must send a clear signal of accountability to those who hold in their hands the future of this treaty, which is crucial for the Union, for our institutions, and for the five hundred million Europeans we represent.
We can no longer be hostages to a few individuals whose sole aim is to undermine the general European interest.

While it is legitimate for some to wish to go no further in European integration, and while it is legitimate to express such reservations, it is equally legitimate to allow those who wish it to move forward.
In general, the processes of ratifying European treaties too often resemble sagas or Pyrrhic victories.
Are we doomed to constantly have to exert pressure to ensure the implementation of a treaty and its institutional reforms?
We must draw lessons from this chaotic ratification process, as well as, of course, from this commitment to negotiation aimed at organizing a second

referendum in Ireland, as if it were normal to make a people vote again after they had expressed their sovereign will, and as if it were normal for consent to a treaty could be used as a bargaining chip.
It is a matter of the coherence and institutional balance of the European venture.
Ratifying a treaty is a minimal requirement of loyalty and cohesion once one has chosen for one's country to join the European Union.
We wanted to send a strong signal to citizens by ratifying the treaty ahead of the June 2009 European elections.
What do you think today's citizen thinks of the Lisbon Treaty, and beyond our internal coherence and legitimacy?
Mr. President, my intervention concerns the deterioration of working conditions in Europe.
Today, nearly half of the workers worldwide earn less than two dollars a day.
Twelve point three million people are still subjected to slavery, and more than two hundred million children are forced to work.
In Europe, the number of working poor increases day by day, and the International Labour Organization records 160 deaths each year linked to lack of prevention.
In this context, I would like to emphasize our responsibility toward all those who are deprived of jobs and social rights, who work under degrading conditions,
of these millions of workers who suffer mentally and physically, sometimes driven to suicide or victims of cancers or chronic illnesses due to their working conditions.
It is time to move beyond the religion of working more, the pursuit of profit, and short-term competition.
The right to decent work and the strengthening of the right to work must be top priorities for the Union over the next ten years.
Madam President, Mr. President of the Council, Mr. President of the Commission, a challenge for humanity, a challenge for future generations.

To rise to the challenge, Copenhagen must enable a comprehensive, single agreement, as our Environment Commissioner, Jolainen Hui, has just emphasized; a coalition of all industrialized countries and emerging economies is essential.

Today, through our debate, as well as the adoption of a resolution by our Parliament, we once again call on the European Union to maintain its leading role in this essential policy of combating climate change.
Let us recall that we must speak with one voice in order to maintain our credibility.
Yes, we must reach an agreement in Copenhagen to limit the increase in global average temperature to a level that does not exceed two degrees above pre-industrial levels.
Yes, we must sign an agreement in Copenhagen to achieve collective reductions in greenhouse gas emissions of thirty percent by 2020 compared to 1990.
But this will not be enough.
It is not only necessary for developed countries to significantly reduce their emissions, but also essential for developing countries to contribute to achieving these goals.

Industrialized countries must therefore provide sufficient, sustainable, and predictable financial and technical support to developing countries to encourage them to commit to reducing their greenhouse gas emissions.
Copenhagen must therefore enable developing countries to transition toward low-carbon models.
Therefore, allow me to address two directions that appear necessary for our future.
First, the establishment at the global level of climate change mitigation financing based on taxing financial transactions.
Next, it seems necessary to implement a border tax adjustment on Europe's borders that would target imports of products manufactured without the slightest regard for environmental protection.

a contractualization and the reversal to the Global South of the carbon border tax collected at Europe's borders, which would be used to finance investments in the infrastructure necessary to combat global warming.

Mr. President, I would like to speak along the same lines as my intervention yesterday regarding the preparation for the Copenhagen summit, and further in light of yesterday’s ECOFIN meeting, which was not successful.
I have addressed to you the responsibility we bear as industrialized nations to ensure that developing countries can keep pace with our efforts to combat climate change.
In Copenhagen, we cannot pretend that we all have the same capabilities, for that would mean starting from the same point and ultimately achieving nothing.
To persuade our development partners to join us, we will undoubtedly need to address the issue of the aid they must receive, and not wait for the outcome of the Copenhagen summit.
At the next European Council, the twenty-seven must absolutely agree on how the European Union envisions assistance to developing countries within the crucial context of climate change.
As I said yesterday, the Ecofin Council of Finance Ministers was unable to reach a common position on this issue.
Yet, we know that this question is absolutely central.

The European Commission has already made proposals, but I absolutely wish for us to take the initiative in driving our partners toward the global solidarity necessary to overcome the challenge of climate change.
I welcome the efforts of the Swedish presidency to achieve an ambitious agreement in Copenhagen, despite the strong wind of pessimism blowing over these future negotiations.
In my view, it is not realistic to admit at this stage that the Copenhagen summit is dull or that it will fail to produce a comprehensive agreement to be ratified by all parties.
Let us not yield too readily to pessimism.
I advocate that at this stage, we should not lower our ambitions, either in terms of objectives or timelines.
We must continue to believe in a comprehensive, ambitious, and binding global political agreement, paving the way for the earliest possible conclusion of a legal treaty.
It is truly too early to discuss the deadline of COP sixteen in December 2010.
We must now translate our rhetoric into genuine political will.
We must clarify our financial commitment and support for developing countries, particularly in terms of technology transfer.
At this stage, a total and collective commitment from the European Union is indispensable.

I also note on paper the recent and exemplary initiative taken by Brazil, South Korea, Indonesia, and Norway, among others, in advancing the negotiations, as they have specified their ambitions to reduce polluting emissions.

While one may understand the flexibility requested by the United States, which has only just begun to address the climate issue, we must nevertheless achieve specific, binding, and ambitious commitments from major polluters such as the United States and China, covering short-, medium-, and long-term periods.
Failing that, we are headed toward climatic, political, and moral disaster.
Madam President, we are all aware, and we have all stated, the importance of the Copenhagen negotiations; but while we all hope for an agreement, we cannot accept an agreement at any price.
If the conditions for an ambitious agreement are not met, I very clearly wish for the European Union to be able to say no and refuse to sign a substandard agreement.
The European populations we represent here are awaiting an agreement, an agreement that would enable us to effectively combat the entire range of climate disruptions we observe daily around us.
They will not be satisfied with publicity stunts, non-specific statements of intent, or non-binding objectives that can be conveniently ignored once the media spotlight has faded.

In Copenhagen, states will need to commit. The European Union, the only continent to have reduced its CO₂ emissions since 1990, the only continent to have set specific, binding, and ambitious commitments for the future, cannot shoulder the burden of the effort alone.

This is why, if other industrialized and emerging countries are unwilling to assume their share of the responsibility, we must fully accept the consequences by imposing at our borders a tax safeguarding our industry against competition from those who choose not to participate in a global agreement.
We Europeans increasingly demand more each day from our industries.
For proof, I cite the climate package that was signed during the French presidency.
For proof, I cite national initiatives, such as the carbon tax recently praised by Mr. Parniaud, and for which I share his satisfaction, that promote the establishment of an environmental tax system.

A sector such as transportation, which is among the major emitters of CO₂, contributes increasingly to the fight against climate change, even though certain modes of transport, such as aviation, emit fewer greenhouse gases.
Indeed, this is a sector that has been severely affected by the repercussions of the crisis.

If, as I hope, maritime and air transport are included in the negotiations in Copenhagen, then let us ensure that any demands placed on European industries are also placed on those of other industrialized countries.
In Copenhagen, world public opinion will be watching closely, and we expect diligent efforts from you. Please complete your task—do not let us down. Thank you.
Madam President, dear colleagues, the Copenhagen summit is a historic opportunity to change the course of the planet.
We must reach decisions even more binding than those adopted in Kyoto.
We too must call for the establishment of an international organization such as the WTO, tasked with regulating issues related to climate change and the environment, and the creation of a fund.
adaptation to climate change, in addition, of course, to public development aid, establishing a maritime and aviation carbon tax, as we must likewise do for financial transactions.
We clearly see the pressing need for global regulation of the environment, just as for financial markets and the internet.
We have the opportunity to begin this march of history with the environment.
Let us make it in the image of our highest human aspirations, otherwise we shall appear as leaders with no grasp of the realities of life. Thank you.

Madam President, Mr. President of the Council, Mr. President of the Commission, dear colleagues, less than two weeks away from the Copenhagen conference, concerns are growing regarding the success of the climate change negotiations.
Some, moreover, are already saying it will be a failure.
Major global powers still hesitate to demonstrate the full ambition that the climate emergency demands.

By adopting tomorrow the resolution proposal on the European Union's strategy ahead of the Copenhagen conference, we, European parliamentarians, must demonstrate that our determination is absolute, and that the conference must lead to an agreement that is not only precise but above all binding.
Moreover, the binding nature of this agreement, once established, must not be subject to challenge or dispute.

One cannot indefinitely postpone decision-making until tomorrow, otherwise it will be too late; hence the necessary creation of a global environmental organization, which today appears not only essential but also urgent, as it will be tasked, under the auspices of the United Nations, with monitoring the implementation of commitments made in Copenhagen.
We must, from Copenhagen onwards, be able to expect from this world greater clarity and responsibility.
I would like to express my support for the joint initiative by the British Labour government and the French government aimed at returning undocumented Afghans to their home country.

Indeed, contrary to what some people have claimed, these individuals had already had their application for political asylum rejected. This bilateral initiative must now be extended at the European level.
Migration pressure is a challenge we must address collectively, and this is precisely the purpose of the migration and asylum pact adopted a few months ago under French presidency.
We must now go further by pooling our resources and organizing joint return flights funded by Frontex.

The decision by the twenty-seven member states to request the Commission to conduct a study on this matter is a first step, and, Mr. Commissioner, I wish the Commission to support this proposal, as combating illegal immigration is a public expectation, particularly among regular immigrants.
Mr. President, I would like to return to a recent event, the Swiss referendum on minarets.
Certainly, this vote took place in a state not belonging to the European Union, but it is clear that it touches upon an issue that is not foreign to us.
I would like to return to this event, as I also see it as an opportunity to discuss secularism, and we never speak enough about secularism within these walls.
It is necessary and urgent that the principle of secularism govern the organization of our society, and I would like to say here some straightforward things.
First of all, the opposite principle of secularism is not religiosity, nor even spirituality.
The principle contrary to secularism is religious communalism, which establishes rules and laws superior to civil laws, the communalism that confines individuals within sub-societal groups.
Our Union, by virtue of its history, is multicultural and multiethnic.
It will become increasingly so, and only secularism can enable individuals
The César programme, aimed at modernizing air traffic management in Europe, is a major initiative.
How will the Commission prepare the next phase of César's redeployment, and in particular the funding of this essential program for both the environment and our scientific and technical standing?
Will the Commission itself follow the recommendations of the report submitted in 2007, according to which a high-level qualified individual should oversee the political follow-up of this programme?
Indeed, it will require overcoming numerous legal, psychological, and technical obstacles, unifying national airspace and enabling air traffic controllers from different countries to work together.
If Europe is unable to unify its skies, how could it possibly build a strong political entity?
Mr. President, Madam Minister, dear colleagues, the importance of the issue of climate change is no longer in question.

These short-, medium-, or long-term impacts are undeniable, even though today's results from the GESC published in a certain national journal are making headlines and undoubtedly strengthen the positions of skeptics.
The Copenhagen summit appeared to the world as a unique opportunity for global leaders to bear witness, take action, and address the consequences of the phenomenon known as climate change.

The European Union was at the forefront of this platform, not only because of the bold legislation we have in place regarding the limitation of our activities' impact on the climate, but also because Europe represents an alliance of countries that can truly make a difference.
Certainly, the outcome of the Copenhagen conference can only leave a taste of dissatisfaction, but I refuse to speak of failure.
The world has agreed on the issue of limiting the increase in temperature to two degrees, even for a country as hesitant as China.
Brazil has just enacted a law aimed at reducing greenhouse gas emissions by nearly thirty-nine percent by 2020, in accordance with its commitments.
Many others have also demonstrated significant ambition regarding the objectives for reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
I am thinking of Japan and Norway.
Moreover, it is also necessary to highlight this very positive aspect: the consensus achieved regarding the urgency of establishing a mechanism to reduce emissions from deforestation.
A specific and unanimous text has been established regarding the implementation of the Direct Plus mechanism to reduce deforestation and thereby enable natural carbon dioxide storage.
Far from a binding agreement clearly in line with a political accord, the outcome of Copenhagen must lead us to question the reasons for such disappointment.
I attended the Copenhagen conference, like many of my colleagues, with the intention of participating, contributing, and witnessing a negotiating Europe, a leading Europe.
My disappointment was great, as Europe clearly did not receive the place it deserved in the negotiations, and all the more so when it comes to the European Parliament.
To assert itself, Europe must demonstrate greater strength on this issue.
The meeting of our ministers on the environment in Seville did not yield results; our officials appeared uncertain and hesitant, and we remain divided on our objectives.
I hope that Madame Edegarde, drawing on her proposals and commitments made before the Environment Committee, will succeed in raising the European Union's ambitions to an appropriate level.

Thank you. I now give the floor to Mr. Baudis. Dear colleagues, in order to move beyond caricatures—some of which we heard this morning—I would like to recall a few objective facts regarding Tunisia, a partner country of the European Union.
Social conditions have improved significantly, with a spectacular rise in average income per capita, and now the vast majority of Tunisians meet the criteria defining membership in the middle class.

Over 90 percent of Tunisians benefit from social coverage, and more than a quarter of Tunisia's budget is allocated to education, placing the country among the first to achieve the Millennium Development Goals for primary education.
Finally, Mr. Michel reminded us just now that women's rights are recognized and guaranteed
At the university, there are more female students than male students; forty of the professors are women; one quarter of the deputies, local officials, or journalists are women.
Many countries could envy such results.
Mr. President, the acts of criminal fanatics who killed Christians in Egypt cannot be attributed to an entire people and its government.
It would be unjust to hold Egypt and the Egyptians responsible for this abominable massacre, the perpetrators of which will be brought to trial.
Let us not confuse the actions of a criminal with the policy of a country, nor equate fanatics with an entire population. Finally, by claiming to interpose ourselves
between the Coptic Christians of Egypt and their Muslim compatriots, we provide extremists with arguments that portray Eastern Christians as agents of the West.
Excuse me, Madam President, Ladies and Gentlemen, what is happening in Greece, in Portugal, tomorrow in Spain or Ireland, is scandalous.

The same Anglo-Saxon agencies that complacently rated unscrupulous issuers and traders of toxic assets, and failed to foresee the crisis, are now displaying the utmost severity toward states that stepped in to rescue a financial sector that did not deserve such aid.

The crisis caused by the irresponsibility of markets and banks, along with this massive assistance, is directly responsible for the worsening of budget deficits and public debt, which these same markets now claim to penalize.
This is what the moralization of capitalism, proclaimed by Mr. Sarkozy, the European Union, and the G20, amounts to.

All the bankers have taken away from the crisis is that the taxpayer is an inexhaustible source of resources, profits, and insurance, since it is widely believed that the scale of this panic originated from a major American bank that received government assistance.

American and two hedge funds of the same nationality seeking to profit simultaneously from the exorbitant interest rates imposed on Greece and from the CDS market—credit default swaps, which are themselves the subject of independent speculation.
It will not be enough to regulate hedge funds, which you fear to do, or the markets for derivative instruments, as Mr. Barnier proposes.
It is unnecessary to create a European economic government.
The twenty-seven are in the same boat anyway, and solidarity is not an option.
It is the global free movement of capital that must be called into question, otherwise there will be no
Mr. President, less than fifteen percent of the original bluefin tuna stock remains today.
Faced with such figures, the solution seemed obvious.
That said, we must not forget the jobs affected by the decisions before us today.
It should be recalled that CITES is not intended to ban fishing, but only the international trade in this fish, of which eighty specimens are exported to Japan.

By safeguarding tuna, we undoubtedly protect a species dangerously overfished, but we also promote a more balanced and sustainable fishing activity, fishing aimed at our domestic market and capable of generating employment.

I support the idea of listing Atlantic bluefin tuna in Appendix I of CITES, and although I believe this decision will be positive, the assistance of the Commission will be necessary to enable a thorough restructuring of the fishing sector.
My question therefore concerns the terms of this support.
France requests an 18-month postponement, accompanied by financial measures, for mariners and shipowners affected by a trade ban.
What is the Commission's vision on this matter?
Moreover, my concern, like that of many of my colleagues, is fairness.
How can we ensure that ships flying the flags of countries such as Tunisia, Libya, or others will enforce the ban on international trade with the same rigor as we do?
What will the new measures for control and sanctions be?

Our policy on the sustainability of economic activities must be concrete, sometimes even unpopular in certain sectors, but I hope the Commission and the Council do not lose sight of the necessary adjustments required for implementing these measures.

Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, as the rapporteur has reported, this proposal for a directive indeed follows upon a vote by the Parliament, and you have also reminded us of this, Commissioner.
Yet, my group remains highly critical of this proposal, as it risks backfiring against those we aim to support.
By seeking to reduce the burdens on businesses, we risk depriving them of the conditions of transparency and trust essential to their management and the vitality of their operations.
First of all, I believe it is essential to be clear about the thresholds and about what is referred to as a micro-entity.
These are SMEs with more than ten employees and an annual turnover of over one million euros.
Regardless of the Member State, this represents a very large majority of companies.
