Certainly, they may be local and scarcely present on the community market, but this does not diminish the fact that the need to establish accounting rules will not be abolished for them.
accounting rules, they will have to face them whenever they wish to deal with a banking institution, with partners, or sometimes negotiate with fiscal, economic, and social institutions.
At that point, accounting rules will be imposed upon them, and they may end up paying more than they would for regular accounting in order to meet criteria they may not necessarily master.
I do not see any progress in imposing accounting requirements outside of any common framework within the European Union.
This is not merely a matter of competition; it is fundamentally a question of economic integration and community law, as well as equal treatment for all enterprises throughout the European Union.
That said, obviously, simplifying accounting obligations for SMEs is a necessity.
Three initiatives, moreover, point in this direction—whether it be the proposal we are discussing today, or the international IF framework.
whether it be the revision of the fourth and seventh accounting directives, for which the European Commission should soon present us with proposals.
These proposals were promised to us for 2010; it appears they have been extended, but I do not believe this extension of the deadline is a sufficient reason for this
rush into things and do half a job, since these three proposals, although they share the same objective, may not have the same structure. It seems to me, Mr. Commissioner,

is clearer for businesses, providing them with a single, comprehensive response that will enable them to reconcile the shared goal of simplifying obligations with the practical realities faced by small businesses that rely on such support.
The current issue is that the European Commission is delaying its proposal for a revision of these directives, and this is detrimental to our deliberations.

I therefore call on the European Commission to accelerate the dialogue on the issue of exemption, which is currently dividing both European institutions and economic circles, and to carry out an impact assessment.

Mr. President, Commissioner, and dear colleagues, as my colleague Elisabeth Morin-Chartier and others have pointed out, the violent storm Xynthia struck several French regions on the 27th and 28th of February, resulting in fifty-three fatalities and extensive material damage.
The storm was of exceptional intensity, causing devastating floods along the French coastline.
After Madeira, Europe is once again affected.
Brittany, the region of my colleague and friend Alain Cadet, has been severely affected.
Three French departments were thus declared to be in a state of natural disaster: Brittany, Poitou-Charentes, and the Pays de la Loire.
Faced with these disasters, the European Union must demonstrate its responsiveness and express its solidarity.
It is not only through words and a resolution, but above all through financial assistance, that we will be able to support the victims of these disasters.

I therefore invite, and my colleague Christophe Béchut joins me in this request, the European Commission to swiftly release the Union's Solidarity Fund to assist the affected regions in coping with the damages.
This disaster underscores the relevance of Michel Barnier's proposal to establish a European civilian intervention force.

Europeans must act together, as disasters are increasing in frequency and affecting our European territories, particularly our most isolated and vulnerable regions, such as the islands in the Indian Ocean and the Caribbean.

Madam President, I too would like to congratulate our rapporteur, Jörg Leichtfried, on his report and his spirit of cooperation on this matter, and I particularly support his proposals regarding the funding of aviation security, since we are discussing aviation security, not aviation safety.
Our committee has been advocating for many years for the principle that stricter security measures must be borne by the Member States, and I fully support our rapporteur on this issue.
Indeed, security concerns not only travelers but all citizens, and it is the responsibility of states to take measures to combat terrorism.
It is therefore unjustified that air passengers alone should bear the cost of security measures at airports and on board aircraft.
This is all the more the case in transport modes such as rail and maritime, where states, depending on the circumstances, fully or partially fund security measures.
I therefore believe we must remain firm in our stance toward the Council and defend to the very end Amendment 32 of the report adopted by our Committee on Transport.
I had another point to address briefly.

Although I support the progress in non-discrimination and transparency that this text will bring, I do not believe the objective of this directive is to establish a single, uniform European system of security charges.
While ensuring compliance with common rules, Member States must be allowed to retain their current system for collecting royalties.

Thus, in countries such as Spain, Italy, or France, royalties are approved by Parliament, and in such cases, the authority responsible for challenging laws must serve as the independent supervisory body.
and this is the purpose of amendment forty, which I hope many of you will support when we vote on it.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, the report I am presenting to Parliament seeks to explain that, in the fight against climate change, agriculture has a role to play and a place to occupy.
This week, nature reminds us that volcanoes in particular can have a significant impact on human activities and on the climate.
But this nonetheless requires that men, and Europe in particular, take their responsibilities in combating what is known as global warming.
My report attempts to demonstrate that agriculture can simultaneously continue reducing its carbon emissions, decrease its consumption of fossil energy, and, above all, on another front,
On the one hand, it has actively contributed to carbon fixation, thereby globally reducing carbon emissions and increasing the amount of carbon fixed and sequestered.
This report attempts to show that in agriculture, we should move away from a compartmentalized approach whereby, whenever we encounter a problem, we simply open a new directive.
on the soil, on water, on pesticides, who knows what else, and to attempt to address the agricultural issue with the ambition of treating it in a comprehensive, systemic manner.
In this approach, we must identify pathways to ensure that agriculture, as I mentioned earlier, not only reduces its energy consumption but also actively contributes to carbon sequestration.
This is particularly the role related to photosynthesis, biomass, and above all, what is close to my heart—soil, and the capacity of European soils to sequester organic carbon.

I believe this path I am opening in this report—and I hope it will be followed during the vote to be held in plenary in Brussels—must enable European agriculture to enter a phase of sustainability, ensuring a

ecological transition toward an agriculture that would position it ahead of numerous other continents or agricultural systems, ensuring it is economically viable, ecologically sound, and, I believe, also socially effective from that point onward.

This report addresses a specific issue—climate change mitigation and agriculture—but it also aspires, I hope, to open up other perspectives to inform, in particular, the debate concerning the future of agricultural policy after 2013.
I am addressing the Commission; I have frequently addressed and will continue to address the agricultural committees; I will also address the various ministers whenever the opportunity arises.
I think we need to make a turn.
This 2013 CAP must be ambitious.
It must be based on objectives that enable citizens to understand why we need a common agricultural policy.
We need a common agricultural policy because Europe's agriculture must ensure food security while also committing to sustainability.
This is at least what I propose in this report, and this is what I hope the Parliament, by a large majority, will follow.

Mr. President, I would first like to emphasize that the precautionary principle implemented by most European governments—closing their airspace in a targeted and temporary manner—was a wise and prudent decision.

The safety of our fellow citizens must take precedence over all other considerations, and under these circumstances, the stance adopted by certain airlines demanding the immediate and total reopening of airspace based on one or two test flights appears at the very least indecent.

It was Mr. Hennin earlier who spoke about the complementarity of transport modes, particularly with regard to trains, and I shall take this opportunity to express how much I regret the air transport paralysis
has been exacerbated in certain countries such as France by the disruption of railway transport, due to strikes carried out under these irresponsible and incomprehensible circumstances.
Returning to our subject, I wish to welcome the Commission's decision to authorize the granting of public aid to airline companies affected by the ongoing disruptions.
It is a sensible decision in a context already marked by crisis, but these aids must take on an exceptional character.

In this regard, I wish that among the criteria used to grant them, consideration be given to the exemplary conduct demonstrated by the airlines in compensating customers who were victims of flight cancellations.
Indeed, it is unacceptable for certain companies to abuse the force majeure clause in order to evade their obligation to compensate passengers arising from Regulation No. 261/2004.
Travelers are victims of the current situation, and if no alternative solution is offered to them, they should not additionally bear the financial cost.
Moreover, travel agencies are exempt from the obligation to refund unused flights to travelers.
This is also not normal.

Companies, like travel agencies, have insurance coverage for exceptional circumstances such as the one we have been experiencing over the past few days, and it is therefore essential to ensure that travelers are properly compensated for flights that have been canceled.
Mr. President, chaos, disorder, paralysis, cacophony, catastrophe, disaster—words abound in the press to describe the closure of European airspace and its consequences.
I will not dwell on what has already been said, particularly regarding the immense financial impact of this crisis—abyssal might even be said—both direct and indirect.

Although I welcome the opportunity to consider granting exceptional public aid to the aviation sector, already strongly endorsed since September 11, 2001, I remain nonetheless perplexed by the European handling of these events.

One. Knowing that seven hundred and fifty European smugglers have been affected, with a significant number still stranded in various corners of the world, knowing that economic losses are exponentially increasing with the passage of time, how can one explain that it took a
Not one, two, three, or four, but five days, for European transport ministers to convene via teleconference to coordinate their actions and decide on the creation of differentiated traffic zones.
Two people do not dispute the necessity of applying the precautionary principle absolutely优先, but today, as we gradually reinstate traffic in certain areas

secured, the weather unchanged, and given that the volcano remains active, one is justified in questioning what additional safety assurances we could not have provided earlier or more swiftly for the passengers.

Thirdly, an evolving action model has been developed: given that volcanic activity can change hourly depending on weather conditions, will the ongoing assessment of corridor safety continue to be updated accordingly?
Should these test flights be carried out by civil aviation and airlines?

Finally, if it persists, worsens, or recurs—which is likely—the management model implemented will need to provide for greater coordination among member states and special operational procedures based on real-time data, as well as improved

coordinating other means of transport under exceptional circumstances, but let us also take into account the necessary coordination of assistance for the tens of thousands of stranded passengers, who likewise have the right to information and support.
Or, to date, only isolated and national initiatives have been taken.

Madam President, I too, following other speakers, would like to express my gratitude to our colleague Mendes de Vigo, who has carried out outstanding work under sometimes heated and always engaging circumstances.
Debates within the Constitutional Affairs Committee lasted a long time, rising to the level of the stakes involved and matching the intensity of interest undoubtedly shown by the.
European parliamentarians on the major issue that concerns them above all, quite legitimately, the composition of our Assembly and the procedures for appointing members of our Assembly.

Once again, the rapporteur has succeeded in bringing together sometimes differing views and contributions, and synthesizing, I would say, in an extremely balanced text, the various opinions and perspectives.
I wanted to thank you for it.

I believe the recommendations contained in these two reports—both the one concerning the convening of the IGC and the one addressing transitional measures for the composition of our Assembly—are marked by realism, pragmatism, and effectiveness.
This is generally what citizens also expect from Europe on other issues.
Mr. President, Mr. Dalli, Commissioner, this draft report, as presented just now by Mr. Peterle, strongly reiterates the guiding principles
Director of Communication at the European Commission, and also drawing inspiration from the resolution of the European Parliament of April 10, 2008, on the fight against cancer in our European Union.

Allow me to support the objectives of the European partnership envisioned by the European Commission to combat cancer more effectively, including the importance of prevention and early detection, the development of a new model for cancer prevention, and above all, the reduction of inequalities among Member States.
I share the concerns and apprehensions expressed in the European Commission's communication and the draft report.

I wish to commend Mr. Peterli, the rapporteur, for the work he has carried out in preparing this report, as well as for the proposed compromise solutions aimed at incorporating the various amendments.

As rapporteur designate for the Socialist and Democratic Group, I wished, among other things, to highlight the following issues: first, the increase in annual cancer-related deaths caused by exposure to carcinogenic agents in the workplace, as well as the importance of improved access to

information on medicines for cancer patients, as well as the implementation of the REACH regulation and the regular updating of substances of very high concern, including carcinogenic substances, and support for initiatives aimed at preventing the importation of goods

containing carcinogenic chemicals and strengthening the oversight of chemical substances within the European Union. Finally, the development of guidelines for a common definition of disability that could include individuals suffering from chronic diseases or cancer.
Here are the questions we have raised, while expressing our support for this draft report.
Madam President, the oil drilling spill in the Gulf of Mexico is a true ecological and economic catastrophe.
It appears that irregularities may have been detected in the issuance by the U.S. Department of the Interior of forest permits.
This would constitute an unforgivable negligence whose consequences are incalculable and which concern not only the United States of America.

Given that hundreds of offshore drilling permits are granted by governments worldwide, if such permits were issued with the same lack of seriousness, it would pose a serious problem for the entire world.
elected representatives from European overseas regions and overseas territories composed entirely or almost entirely of islands, numbering nearly one hundred and thirty,
In total, spread across the three continents, I must express here my deep concern regarding our islands in the face of offshore oil exploitation projects.
This is why it is imperative that the European Union, as a model to follow, re-examines with the utmost rigor the safety and prevention measures on its own offshore oil platforms.
Moreover, accidental or operational marine oil spills, not necessarily caused by Europe, are estimated at several tens of thousands of tonnes each year.

Would it not be appropriate for the European Union to take initiatives so that offshore platforms would henceforth fall under an undisputed international scientific authority, which could be established in the form of an international body?

Following Copenhagen and in preparation for Cancun, the European Union would do itself a great honor by launching genuine global governance for the research, protection, and exploitation of deep-sea resources.
Continuing the Barcelona process, the Union for the Mediterranean presents an opportunity for the development of our relations with the countries of the eastern and southern Mediterranean basin.
As Vincent Payon's excellent report underscores, we cannot disregard either the potential of this region or the dangers it entails.
Yet, let us be clear about it: since the bombardment of Gaza and the ongoing colonization of Palestinian territories, the Union for the Mediterranean is at a standstill.
The political aspect is stalled.
It is not even certain that the summit scheduled for next June in Barcelona will actually take place.
I would not be surprised if we learned in the coming hours or days that he is on an unprecedented journey.
We must not give up, but the European Union must take greater responsibility in seeking a political solution.
We cannot allow the United States to have a monopoly on the political management of the crisis in this region, which is ours, while merely contributing to its development.
The development and stability of the Mediterranean are essential to our security and growth, and for this purpose, the Union for the Mediterranean remains the best instrument at our disposal.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, this is an extremely important issue concerning consumer protection.

A recent survey indicates that Europeans perceive the food security situation as having deteriorated over the past ten years, and one in three consumers remains skeptical about the quality of food products.
Therefore, updating, simplifying, and clarifying food labelling across the European Union is an obligation for better consumer protection.
Allow me to address two points.
Regarding the color code, we support this code provided it is accompanied by a reference to nutritional values expressed as percentages.
I am opposed to mandatory nutritional labeling for wines and distilled beverages.
We wish to establish a regulation specific to wines and spirit drinks within the next three years, rather than the five years proposed by the European Commission.
That is what I wished to point out, Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner.
Madam President, High Representative, this attack on civilian vessels by Israeli commandos undoubtedly requires an impartial and credible investigation.
But what credibility could we grant to a Commission that would be both judge and party?
This attack occurred in international waters, thus involving the international community, and particularly concerns Europe's Mediterranean coastal states.
Europe should speak out more and become more actively involved in seeking a political solution based on two states, each with Jerusalem as its capital.

Europe is making considerable financial efforts to support the development of Palestinian territories, but unfortunately, it is too absent from the political process that should lead to the establishment of a Palestinian state.
Our financial and budgetary goodwill is not enough.
We must also express a political will and take the necessary measures to make ourselves heard.
The brutal boarding of the Mavi Marmara by the Israeli army has provoked outrage and condemnation.
emotion upon seeing the nine victims of the civilian assault, condemnation because this boarding in international waters constitutes a violation of international law.
Doing so, the Israeli government penalizes its own people, because ultimately, as extremism strengthens, we see the ultimate goal of security in the region receding further.
Yes, more than ever, the Israeli government must abandon the policy of force.
I wish to note, however, that the intense media coverage surrounding the Mavi Marmara flotilla, orchestrated from Turkey, casts doubt on the exclusively humanitarian nature of this operation.
An investigation appears indispensable to shed light on the assault, as well as on the preparations that preceded this expedition.
And I hope that the Government of Israel agrees to include substantial international participation in this investigation, so that the paths to justice in this region may finally be restored.
Madam President, High Representative, I welcome the new momentum generated by the launch of the partnership for modernization and its pragmatism.
This new partnership, even if it achieved nothing else, would at least have the merit of highlighting just how indispensable Russia is as a geopolitical partner for the European Union.
I also welcome the positive remarks regarding cooperation with Russia on crisis management.
A few remarks, however.

While the requirements of the Union are consistently emphasized, the interests or positions of its partner appear systematically underestimated or dismissed, whether in the domain of energy or in how it assesses the Eastern Partnership and the Atlantic aspirations of its closest neighbors.

I also note that the firmness displayed by most Member States of the Union in their statements, as well as the severity of their condemnations of human rights violations, appear to be reserved exclusively for Russia.

The remarks are more restrained, diplomatic language even more muted, as though referring to China, for instance, to these systematic violations of the rule of law—just consider the Laogai, this global factory in terms of minimal European social standards, while our French or European workers remain unemployed.

Allow me to remind you of the saying of Augusta: "Ascend to the peaks by narrow paths," for this is how I view Russia's current efforts, freed from the criminal poison of Marxism, which for so many decades bled and imprisoned this land.
The current Russian Federation has managed to lift the head of an entire nation, despite the significant difficulties and challenges that remain to be met.

So, a little modesty, gentlemen of European integration, especially when the super European state itself fails to achieve any of its noble goals, despite increasingly costly resources and the growing dominance of the states of Western Europe, as well as Central and Eastern Europe.
I cannot help but recall that this Assembly showed greater indulgence toward the Communist Soviet Union than toward Russia.

Indeed, it is often the very same individuals who yesterday advocated for the unilateral disarmament of the West and for participation in Mr. Gorbachev’s "common house" who now express the most virtuous indignation and the greatest moral reservations regarding the partnership with the Russian Federation.

On this subject, I must inform you—should you not yet be aware—of new historical facts that will emerge as a result of the gradual translation of Soviet secret archives, which a courageous man, Mr. Pavel Stroilov, managed to obtain—archives that have already yielded and will continue to yield numerous revelations.

unpleasant surprise for the conventional wisdom regarding the reality of the end of the Cold War, which was genuinely Mr. Gorbachev, and the betrayal by today's powerful Europeanists who enthusiastically sought to enable certain Soviet projects at the time.
At the risk of repeating myself, Russia is an essential and strategic partner for European states, and our relations must be strengthened for the benefit of all parties.

This country is far closer to us in terms of culture, civilization, history, geography, mutual interests, or shared risks than certain states you are nonetheless preparing to admit to the European Union.
President of the Council, it is often said that necessity makes law.
Faced with the crisis, one truth stands out—a necessity that has become inevitable: to innovate, to be creative.

In this regard, I welcome the step taken by the Council, a step I hope will ultimately become a giant leap toward genuine economic governance, for let us not be mistaken—mere coordination of economic policies will remain insufficient.
Might we achieve a genuine budgetary federation, not imposed by circumstances, but because we ourselves have decided upon it?
Let us return to the Council's decisions, which nonetheless please me.
Strengthening the preventive component of the Stability and Growth Pact is a positive development, but there can be no genuine budgetary discipline without sanctions for those who fail to comply.
They have been strengthened but not yet clarified, which is regrettable.

Otherwise, I shall express my satisfaction, among other things, regarding the decisions taken, such as the much-publicized competitiveness assessment dashboard, since that is precisely what this is about—reconnecting with competitiveness and sustainable growth for all.
We also welcome the proposed establishment of a banking tax, the acceptance of taxing financial transactions, and the acceleration of financial regulation reforms.
Mr. President of the Council, all these decisions and guidelines seemed, just a few months ago, or even just a few weeks ago, unrealistic, illusory, or truly hypothetical.
You're right, it's true, it represents a profound cultural shift on the scale of Europe's incremental policy approach.
That said, it is now imperative that, in October, states implement these guidelines in a very concrete manner.

It will now be up to the Council whether this progress represents a historic leap forward or merely an empty wish, something we will all bitterly regret, particularly the majority of citizens who will have to endure its consequences every day. Thank you.

Mr. President, we have already discussed this matter in the Conference of Presidents; many groups are divided on the Gallo report, and clearly, the Parliament and all the groups still need to discuss it further, as there are contradictory points that the groups have been unable to resolve.
That is why we requested to postpone the debate and vote on the Gallo report, namely in September two, which will require consultation with the Conference of Presidents.
Therefore, it is a very simple matter.
Since the fruit is not yet ripe, let it ripen over the summer and let's discuss it in September.
Madam President, I must admit I find it difficult to follow my colleagues, who so eagerly display their strength and claim the monopoly of our legitimacy over the other institutions of the Union.
Unfortunately, their stance is often laughable.
But when the members of this household, through hard work and conviction, assert a well-reasoned position, when
We defend what resembles a European general interest against rigid states, and the situation changes completely.
In this debate on European supervision, the Parliament has played this role.
Regarding the European Systemic Risk Board, of which I am the shadow rapporteur for my group, I have advocated, like the rapporteur, Ms. Goulard, an ambitious approach.
I have advocated mechanisms ensuring a certain level of effectiveness, and I have emphasized the primacy of the European Central Bank.
Why?
because the European Central Bank is indeed the only community institution within this framework, and because it has demonstrated its seriousness.
By Christian charity, I shall refrain from mentioning the names of all the institutions and all these great figures who suddenly vanished during the storm,
We may consider ourselves fortunate to have had at least one captain on board at the height of the crisis.
I shall therefore be reassured tomorrow to see macroprudential supervision anchored in a strong community institution, whereas microprudential supervision will inevitably remain fragmented.
In these troubled times, the European Systemic Risk Board must get to work as soon as possible.
The future will show whether adjustments will be necessary, but it is essential to start on sound foundations, as systemic risks affect the entire European and global banking sector.
Mr. President, the final agreement reached on the revision of the directives concerning industrial emissions is more than acceptable, and I commend the work of compromise that has been achieved.

This final package incorporates the key points of the position I have advocated, together with several of my colleagues, within the Environment Committee, aiming to make this text an ambitious yet realistic piece of legislation.
First, maintaining a certain degree of flexibility for businesses and competent authorities in granting exemptions.
Next, I would like to welcome the new version of the ESN, so controversial and now limited to certain sectors, which appears much more achievable.

It is also important to emphasize that the agreement reached enables better alignment with the ETS directive and definitively removes from the text of the new directive the somewhat irrelevant notion of establishing a separate market for SO₂ and NOx allowances.
Some reservations to bear in mind, however, particularly those concerning the ground.
Since the soil directive remains pending, this important issue will unfortunately be addressed only by those Member States that have their own legislation in place.
Tomorrow, Parliament must adopt the elements of the agreement reached following the trilogues.

Enormous effort has been made from all sides to prevent this text from being referred back for conciliation—a text, I must emphasize, of crucial importance, both for industry and for European environmental policy.
Madam President, each day brings its share of information revealing the extent of the ecological disaster caused in the Gulf of Mexico by the offshore platform rupture.
But let us not forget that other disasters of this nature are occurring elsewhere.
I am thinking in particular of what is happening today and has been happening for a long time already in Nigeria.

That is why, in addition to the moratorium on new drilling, I believe there is a need to promote stricter regulations regarding platform management, not only European rules but also international ones.
As for the European Union, I believe it is necessary for it to proactively establish measures to combat this disaster, and I think it is obvious
as well as all this argues, as Mrs. Damanaky stated, for a firm and resolute choice in favor of clean and renewable energies,
And the European Union should set an example in this regard.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, reopening negotiations with Mercosur at a time when the European Union is preparing to redefine its agricultural policy reveals a dangerous lack of coherence on the part of the Commission.
Europe already imports five hundred thousand tonnes of beef.
A new opening of our market will come at the expense of farmers in Europe's disadvantaged regions, without assurance of compliance with sanitary and social standards.
We cannot accept this.
Europe imports thirty-eight million tonnes of soy and animal feed.
These imports from Brazil and Argentina account for more than nineteen million hectares.
They are concentrated in the hands of three companies: Cargill, ADM, and Bunge.
Between 2001 and 2004, soybean production destroyed more than 1.2 million hectares of tropical and equatorial forests.
It is one of the primary causes of the increase in greenhouse gas emissions and the loss of biodiversity.
The European Parliament has decided to produce a report on the recovery of vegetable protein autonomy for livestock feed.
Environmentally responsible agriculture and the fight against climate change dictate this choice.
These efforts will be rendered futile by this proposed bilateral agreement.
Once again, the European Commission is sacrificing its agriculture and farmers in order to secure uncertain concessions for service companies.
These concessions will in no way benefit the smallholder farming families of Mercosur, but only the interests of agro-industrial groups.
To be convinced, it is sufficient to read the report by the Tyson group, a major American company dominating the international meat market,
who announced having invested in Brazil, to quote, and this is in his report, using this country as an export platform for Europe.
Other companies, such as the Doux group, had already anticipated this approach by acquiring the Brazilian group Frango Soul in 1998.
Many poultry farmers, particularly in Brittany, had suffered from this operation.
Europe appears determined to continue its policy of offshoring by intensifying its attacks
against the peasants. We must stop
Mr. President, many colleagues are concerned about the potential consequences for European agriculture arising from the reopening of negotiations on an association agreement with Mercosur.
My group and I share these concerns.

It is indeed to be feared, and as a deputy representing an ultra-peripheral region, I have firsthand experience with banana agreements. It is therefore to be feared that an agreement with Mercosur would deal a severe blow to many European agricultural sectors.
This is why an impact assessment is absolutely necessary.

It is also feared that this agreement may further, and perhaps excessively, orient Mercosur's agriculture toward a productive model, to the detriment of the smallholdings that exist in these countries.
An equilibrium must therefore be found so that this agreement does not transform the Latin American and European continents into superpowers essentially grounded in the dogma of mercantilism.
Mr. President, I would simply like to say that we may not have properly set today's agenda.

If we allow free trade agreements to bring products into the territory of Europe that do not comply with either our social standards or our environmental standards, then the reform of the CAP will be pointless.
Very clearly, we cannot remain in a hypocrisy where, on one hand, we impose environmental, sanitary traceability, and safety requirements,
to our farmers, on the grounds that consumers must be protected, and on the other hand, allowing products to enter our continent freely without adhering to these standards.
The question underlying the negotiation with Mercosur is precisely that one.

And the way in which matters were initiated, regardless of what we are told about Parliament having been very well informed and about proceedings having taken place with the utmost transparency and clarity, does not lead us to view favorably a negotiation that unfolds at the same time as ongoing negotiations within the WTO.
On the grounds of consistency, consumer protection, and the defense of our farmers, I do not believe we should continue along this path.

Madam President, I would also like to commend the work carried out by rapporteur José Bové, to acknowledge the Commissioner, and to recognize the intervention of Mr. Dess, who preceded me, on the objectives of this report.

I believe it is very important to keep clearly in mind that in the debate we are having, we are addressing a problem related to price volatility in agriculture and especially to the fact that when prices
They quickly affect distribution prices, but when prices fall at the production level, there are no corresponding reductions for consumers.
There is indeed an organizational issue within the supply chain, and there is indeed a problem regarding the distribution of what is called added value within the supply chain.

I believe José Bové's report offers, on several points, perspectives that must be highlighted, and here I am addressing the Commission directly in an effort to break out of this fundamentally negative spiral.
The first, of course, is the organization of the supply chain and the balance of power within the supply chain. And therefore,
What proposals do we put forward to ensure that producers, when gathered and organized, have greater influence within the supply chain to strengthen their negotiating position?
That is the question you raised, Mr. Commissioner of the interprofessional bodies, but it is also one that will be addressed on the basis of the contractual framework that will be established.
I argue that this contractual basis should have a European framework, as set out in the report.
One cannot allow each country to solve this problem on its own.
There must be a European framework.
It must be flexible, but it must exist.
It is a proposal, an inseparable path for me.
Secondly, of course, the issue of price transparency.
And there, I am in favor of establishing this observatory, which is absolutely necessary in order to inform consumers about the evolution of prices.
I support the proposals that have been made, and I hope, as Mr. Dess said, that there will be a majority in favor of adopting them.
Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, today I would like to welcome this debate, which once again underscores the need for the Union to assess and appreciate its regional diversity.
At this time of negotiating the new framework programmes for 2014, I believe it is important to emphasize the arrangements that should benefit specific regions.
Questions of territorial continuity and cohesion must be at the heart of our concerns.
But the development of mountainous, insular, or sparsely populated regions requires more than just a set of derogatory measures.
We need a horizontal approach
We must establish the tools capable of synergistically integrating all areas of community action in order to enhance their added value.
Moreover, as a representative of the outermost regions, I eagerly await the new European Union strategy for the OMRs, which the Commission's services promised us for early 2011.
Madam President, Commissioner, my dear colleagues first of all, I align myself with my colleague's position regarding the Council's absence from this meeting of the Parliament.
I find that a little careless on the part of the Council, and I want to say that as well.
As the next summit approaches on October 6, China and the European Union must absolutely and resolutely strive together to make progress on the issue of climate change.
Last year, at the same time, we were already engaged in this very chamber urging Europe to do everything possible to achieve an ambitious and binding agreement in Copenhagen.
It has been observed that the outcome did not meet our expectations and ambitions.
Thus, concerns one year later are the same, or even much more intense.

In order to be able to compel or persuade our partners today, we must demonstrate that our determination remains absolutely intact, for example by showing that we are providing ourselves with the means to fulfill our commitments to countries in the Global South.
To this end, the establishment of innovative financing mechanisms has become a necessary imperative—I heard President Sarkozy's speech at the UN yesterday.
Therefore, for example, the introduction of a carbon border tax is not out of the question.

It would act as an incentive mechanism for countries that are not going far enough in their climate commitments, and far more than a mere threat, serving as a means to protect our jobs and businesses, particularly in better addressing the post-crisis period.

Madam President, in my view, the necessity to enhance family leave is not justified. Raising the minimum threshold for maternity leave is a step forward, a benefit, and we should not be entirely demagogic in comparing economic impacts with qualitative benefits that are difficult to quantify.

But the problem is essentially twofold: first, the economic context, which is indeed significant, yet insufficient to justify leaving millions of families stranded for decades; second, the legal gaps in the report, as the text itself contains several types of family leave whose legal foundations are incompatible.
Consider the adoption leave provided for in the text, on the same footing as maternity and paternity leave.
Personally, as an adoptive mother and on behalf of all those I represent, I appreciate the intention to grant rights equivalent to those of biological mothers.
I am in fact the additional small package that Madame Morin mentioned to be added to the Estrella report.

If the objective is truly to strengthen the health and rights of women, of all women, in the labor market, adoptive women, who have become mothers just like any other mothers, are entitled to the same rights and the same protection at work.
They are mothers like any other, fully so—whether they adopt a child under or over twelve months, please note there is no discrimination, as clearly stated in the text.
Regarding adoption, I regret that the text was not explored in greater depth.
It was not even informed by the Rambol impact study.
This is not well developed, which is clearly a weakness.

Nevertheless, despite these reservations, I will support Mrs. Estrella's report, as beyond economic considerations, there are men and women who must not only better assume their parental responsibilities in a society of

increasingly disengaged from the education of these young people, but moreover, because it is our duty to ensure that no one has to choose between sacrificing their children for their work or sacrificing their work for their children.
Finally, we do not sit on the Council, but in Parliament. If we, directly elected representatives, are not ambitious, then tell me who will be.
Madam President, Commissioner, President of the Council, I would like to intervene without going back over the debate as it has unfolded so far.
We discussed the 2011 budget under the constraints we are aware of, namely the deep crisis currently affecting Europe, with national public deficits that may lead to two hypotheses.
These national public deficits constrain the European deficit, or these national deficits must provide a new direction and renewed impetus at the European level.
That is precisely the question.
I ask you, Mr. Commissioner, above all to try to consider, when discussing a budgetary revision, when we begin to talk about budgetary perspectives beyond 2013, not to
remain completely immersed in the context of the crisis, unless I am to tell myself and tell all the citizens of Europe that, definitively, Europe will remain in crisis for a long time.
Our entire challenge is indeed to manage the crisis and reduce our deficits and debt, but it is also to prepare for exiting the crisis.
And it is there that the budgetary question arises.

We will not be able to overcome the crisis, as Joseph Dole stated immediately, unless the European budget, because it is European, is able to provide us with the means to regain today what we lack in terms of growth, job creation, innovation, and research.

That is the issue at stake, and it is to this honor that we must address the budget—rather than, as too often happens, continually incorporating the questions of crisis and deficits, where the Council excels.

I noted that in Deauville, Angela Merkel and Nicolas Sarkozy even announced in advance a reform of the treaties, without having discussed anything whatsoever with Mr. van Rompuy, in order to sanction all states that fail to respect budgetary discipline tomorrow.

This is where we stand today at the Council level, while the Parliament is asking for only one thing—particularly of the Commission—to try to advance the European project, and this is being done through the budget.
You have noted a number of points on which we completely agree.
More flexibility is needed.
Of course, we must collectively find a way to establish much greater budgetary flexibility, as this is a matter of necessity and concerns Europe's ability to respond promptly.
It also involves, of course, setting priorities and organizing the efficiency of the European budget.
We will all agree on this, completely in agreement—it is a necessity.
But where we must be clear with one another is that we need a schedule, we need to jointly set objectives, Commissioner. It cannot remain merely words, mere commitments, mere

texts which are, moreover, very interesting and which I largely share, practically one hundred percent. But now it will be necessary to get down to concrete matters, and in particular to address a crucial issue around which we keep circling: own resources.

How do we secure our own resources for Europe tomorrow—not for Europe alone, but for all European citizens—so that together we can rediscover the path to growth and forge an even stronger future for Europe?
It is your turn, Mr. Commissioner.
Mr. President, I would like to focus my remarks on the preparation for the upcoming G20 summits.

Everything has already been said by previous speakers regarding European economic governance, the need to strengthen European economic governance, to reinforce the Stability and Growth Pact, and to involve the European Parliament and of course also national parliaments more effectively in the process.
I would like to briefly mention two challenges facing our nations and the members of the G20, which, in my view, must be addressed within the framework of the upcoming G20 meetings.
the challenge of currency wars and that of commodity price volatility.
I believe that on these two issues, which constitute real threats to global growth and cause very significant imbalances on our planet, the European Union must

The European Union manages to organize itself, adopt common positions, and equip itself to speak with one voice at the upcoming G20 summits in Seoul and those to follow, and more broadly on the global stage. Thank you.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, I would first like to congratulate our colleague Diogo Feijo on the quality of the report and on the ambitious proposals it contains.
It also demonstrates that the European Parliament is fully asserting its role in a debate that is decisive for Europe's future, and we can only congratulate ourselves on this.
Moreover, the Greek crisis served as a revelation of the dysfunctional elements undermining the economic governance of the European Union.
I note Mr. Feyot's proposal to establish a permanent mechanism for financial stability.
We must also address the problem at its root.
To this end, I believe the solution also lies in strengthening the Stability and Growth Pact, and in particular its sanctions.
This is essential if we are to achieve a sustainable improvement in the budgetary situation of the states, which is not always popular.
It is burdensome, but we have no choice.

I believe the report clearly highlights the challenges posed by the marine environment in terms of fishing, aquaculture, transportation, tourism, energy, mineral and biological resources, research, combined with climate change.
It is a considerable development potential that must be secured, protected, and sustainably enhanced, as humanity's future depends on it.
The report also rightly notes that the European Union is the world's leading maritime power and that its overseas regions provide it with the largest exclusive economic zone in the world.
Given such a situation, it is therefore up to the European Union to implement an ambitious policy, a major maritime policy.
If not the world's leading maritime power, then who else will do it in its place?
But it must be acknowledged that the resolution does not go very far in this direction.
One cannot claim leadership in global politics with fifty million euros.
Finally, although the outermost regions have a recognized strategic position since they are present in all oceans, it would have
It was prudent to assign them a major role in a comprehensive ocean strategy, which, unfortunately, was not retained in the report, and I regret that. Thank you, Mr. President.
Madam, Commissioner, I would like to return to the anti-counterfeiting agreement currently being negotiated, particularly with the United States.
This agreement also raises questions in the United States, particularly among officials at the United States Patent Office.
Moreover, seventy-five American law professors have recently sent a letter to President Obama.
He considers that ACTA, being merely an executive agreement, would be contrary to the United States Constitution.
While, as you know, the European Parliament is preparing to adopt a resolution on the ACTA agreement, this agreement continues to raise major concerns and remains far from being universally accepted.
among our fellow citizens, I believe it would be helpful to receive from our American counterparts a very clear response on two points: Can ACTA be ratified by the United States?
Is ACTA perceived by them as a binding or a voluntary agreement?
Indeed, an anti-counterfeiting agreement from which China, India, and Brazil are already absent, and which would not be implemented by the United States, would lose much of its credibility.
Thank you.

Mr. President, throughout this difficult delivery of the alternative investment funds directive, I have consistently advocated for the principle of equal treatment between fund managers, regardless of whether they are based in the European Union or not.
This principle sounds like an obvious truth, but this obviousness has encountered countless difficulties.
The result presented to us today is satisfactory in nature.
I thank the negotiators, and above all our rapporteur, Mr. Gauzès, who, following this particularly painful experience for him, deserves a substantial maternity leave.
I would especially like to emphasize that the issue of treating third countries must still be viewed from two perspectives, whether one is inside or outside the European Union.
Thanks in particular to the European Parliament, all funds sold within the European Union will effectively be subject to comparable regulation.
One can therefore speak, to a greater or lesser extent, of fair playing conditions.

It remains that, during the transitional period, non-European entities will benefit from the national placement regime, while funds and managers within the European Union will be subject to the directive, which is generally stricter.
The situation is different on markets located outside the European Union. There, European funds and their managers will remain subject to the directive, whereas funds from third countries will not.
It would therefore be much more difficult to sell European products around the rest of the world, as they would be less flexible and more expensive.
We must be aware of this disadvantage, even though it was partly unavoidable.

Regarding depositaries, we should also recognize that we will make the process more demanding, particularly due to the new responsibilities they will assume; the increased requirements should lead to substantial changes in their profession.

Mr. President, I am not saying this to demonstrate my opposition, but to emphasize that this new regulation is by no means trivial, and that its implementation, while necessary, will also entail difficulties of which we must be aware.

Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, particularly at the beginning of the 21st century, patients need appropriate information in order to be fully involved in their care pathway, to know where to seek care, to decide on the most suitable treatment in agreement with healthcare professionals, and to strictly follow the prescribed treatment.
Today, patients demand independent, comparative, and personalized information tailored to their individual needs.
Unfortunately, the revision proposals for the directive submitted by the European Commission did not adequately address the needs expressed by European citizens.
These proposals opened the door to direct promotion by pharmaceutical companies, which appears to us as nonsensical in terms of addressing patients' actual needs.
This operation holds no interest for either citizens or member states.
On the contrary, it means, unlike additional bureaucracy, an increase in expenditures and exposing patients to risks.
For these reasons, we had initially submitted a proposal to reject these texts, thereby requesting the Commission to revise its draft.

This approach, also adopted by my colleague Carl Schlitter from the Greens group, has enabled our rapporteur, Mr. Fechner, whom I salute, to carry out a significant revision of the Commission's proposals.

At the same time, we had submitted approximately sixty amendments aimed at further protecting the health of our citizens and facilitating patients’ access to independent, comparative information tailored to individual needs.

The adoption of these amendments in the Environment Committee enabled a stronger emphasis on patients' rights to information, rather than making the provision of information an option for the pharmaceutical industry.
Given the compromise amendments achieved, we have decided to withdraw our proposal to reject the texts, as the substance of the text has evolved in a positive direction over the past few months.
The draft report as amended has succeeded in offsetting the European Commission's initial proposals.

However, we will continue to oppose the instrumentalization of healthcare professionals in distributing to their patients brochures and information provided by pharmaceutical companies, without any oversight by health authorities.
Likewise, we will oppose the authorization of industrial information campaigns, particularly with regard to vaccination.
Information cannot freely come from pharmaceutical companies.

Experience shows that we must be extremely vigilant, and indeed, to conclude, what happened with the Mediator medications illustrates just how vigilant we must be and ensure that this balance point, which we have sought through this report proposal, is maintained.

Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, Madam Minister, unity and realism will be reflected— at least I hope so— in tomorrow’s vote on our resolution regarding the sixteenth Conference of the Parties on climate change.

I therefore hope that our commissioner in charge will be able to pursue the practical objectives we collectively need to achieve, as well as defend the indispensable conditions set by Europe in this regard.
In these negotiations, we must also keep in mind the legitimate expectations of fairness held by our fellow citizens and our industries.
It is the world that must combat climate change, not Europe alone.
Thus, recognizing the importance of intellectual property that is too little protected will be essential.

Green technologies must benefit the greatest number, but we must not, for that reason, undervalue our current and future knowledge, even in the face of this indispensable fight against climate change.

Similarly, requesting common calculation methods for emissions, as well as the verification and publication of data, so that all emitting industries are subject to the same standard, appears to be a perfectly legitimate request, not a negotiating tactic.
Finally, more controversial—and my colleague raised this point earlier—gaps in the current system of clean development mechanisms will also need to be addressed.
Dear colleagues, we are all fully aware, regretfully, that the real negotiating power does not lie with the European Parliament.

But the determination of many European parliamentarians, including myself, to work towards and contribute to a positive outcome of this conference must be encouraged, not overlooked, as we have experienced in recent days.
If Europe wishes to be strong within international dialogues, its legislators must likewise be strong.
Madam President, the European Union often presents economic partnership agreements as an opportunity for African countries.
Thus, does she despair at not seeing these agreements signed as soon as possible?
But the question that concerns us is why these countries do not rush to sign agreements that would be so advantageous to them.
Clearly, opinions differ regarding the quality of these agreements, and as long as these differences in assessment persist, the ACP countries will hesitate to sign them.
Would it not be appropriate to go further in dialogue and understanding by listening to the concerns and expectations of the ACP countries?
These ones wish to discuss development strategy, whereas Europe often responds merely with market opening.
It is this listening that the Parity Parliamentary Assembly ACP-European Union imposes upon itself.
I believe other European bodies should adopt the same approach if we are to establish a genuine strategic partnership between Africa and the European Union.
Christians are undoubtedly one of the most persecuted religious groups in the world.

In the Middle East, they are perceived by certain extremist groups—as I quote—as legitimate targets, with the objective being their outright departure from this region, which gave birth to Christianity and where their historical presence is essential to a certain balance.
The events at the cathedral in Baghdad are merely the climax of a situation we have passively observed for far too long.
The European Union, as a global actor and commercial partner of numerous countries in this region, must strongly express its indignation in light of the situation.
Discrimination of any kind cannot be tolerated.
Democracy will only prevail in this region when the most fundamental human rights, such as religious freedom, are respected.
Dear colleagues, we are all deeply concerned by the serious crisis currently taking place in Côte d'Ivoire.

It is clear that the decision by this country's Constitutional Council to challenge, in violation of the electoral code, the election results officially proclaimed by the independent electoral commission has been unanimously condemned by the international community.

The European Union, the United Nations, the African Union, the Parity Parliamentary Assembly of the European Union-ACP, the COAS, and soon certainly the European Parliament, are demanding respect for the official proclamation of the

the election results and the installation of the new President of the Republic, as recognized by the Independent Electoral Commission, which, I remind you, is an institution validated by the United Nations.
All political groups in the European Parliament have expressed, in a joint statement, their agreement on these essential points.

They also called for halting any escalation of tensions within the country that could lead to civil war, which would represent a human, social, and economic disaster for Côte d'Ivoire—something that must obviously be avoided at all costs.

Madam President, I would like in turn to thank our four rapporteurs, who have carried out outstanding work and have significantly enhanced the European Parliament's contribution to the text initially proposed by the Commission.
The citizens' initiative that we at the PPE are preparing to vote for with enthusiasm represents a major advancement of the Lisbon Treaty.
Let us not be mistaken: this instrument can truly transform the political landscape of Europe, provided it is used with both responsibility and courage—I repeat, responsibility and courage.
It can help bring European citizens directly into the European political debate.

It can help foster a European public opinion, that is, men and women who are citizens of Europe, engaging in joint debate, taking collective positions, and coming together on issues that are truly European.
But let us not disappoint our fellow citizens.
Urge our member states to take swift implementation measures where necessary; let us not disappoint our fellow citizens, for this concerns the strengthening of European democracy.
I wished, in turn, like several of our colleagues, to address the issue of the persecution of Christians throughout the world, particularly Christians of the East.
There is no need to recall the horrible acts recently committed.
I would like to solemnly emphasize that defending Christians in the East as well as everywhere in the world, advocating for religious freedom, the diversity of
beliefs, it means advocating for freedom, democracy, and human rights, which are values at the heart of the European project and which, of course, we are all convinced here are universal values.
It is truly time now, beyond indignation, emotion, and solemn condemnations—of course—to move from words to actions.
I urgently call upon the authorities of the European Union, the High Representative for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy, as well as all our policymakers, the Ministers of Foreign Affairs,
undertake specific measures, implement a precise, concrete action plan to make respect for religious diversity and the fight against all forms of religious persecution a priority. Thank you.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, High Representative, we all agree that the situation in the Sahel region has considerably deteriorated.
and allowing a zone of impunity to take root at Europe's ports is a danger we must resolutely and immediately confront.
Extremist groups are multiplying and threatening the lives of civilian populations.
On my part, I regret the loss of human life and the abductions, and I am concerned about European nationals present on the ground.
To date, Morocco had served as a barrier against all kinds of trafficking originating from the Sahel.
Now, drug, arms, and human traffickers circumvent Morocco by passing through Mauritania to the Canary Islands, en route to the European Union.
It seems to me that it is the responsibility of the European Union to act in order to assist its member states in putting an end to these threats.
Europe's security is at stake on our territory, as well as in its immediate neighborhood.
It is time for us to act, and I call on the Commission and the Council to implement an action plan to help restore security in the Sahel.
That has already been said, but I join the expressions of sympathy and support that have already been extended to the peoples of Tunisia and Egypt.
It represents a symbol of hope for all defenders of freedom.
A thought also for the victims.

I would now like to say that the European Union, which, as has been noted, has been criticized for its lack of response, must stand firmly alongside the peoples of Tunisia and Egypt to help them successfully open their countries to reform and establish democracy.

The European Union has decided to support Tunisian leaders in initiating a peaceful transition, preparing for the upcoming elections to achieve freedom, and enabling the Tunisian people to attain prosperity through development and social peace, creating an economy that will provide employment opportunities for youth.

I would conclude by saying that the Union for the Mediterranean faces a significant challenge, as the Jasmine Revolution has triggered a wave across all the countries of the Maghreb, the Middle East, and the Near East.
It must implement a robust strategy toward these countries.

But beware, in the face of this legitimate democratic movement, Europe must find a balance between non-interference in internal affairs, support for the legitimate aspirations of peoples, and stability.
Commissioner, my colleagues have reminded him of this on numerous occasions.

At a time when Europe is questioning a common strategy for its raw material supply, and particularly for rare earths, we cannot overlook the immense potential represented by the intelligent valorization of our waste.

Strengthening European standards to improve the functioning of collection chains, address issues of leakage or illegal export of waste, and increase our collection targets should be at the heart of this legislation.
Let us not forget the main actor in the success or failure of this regulation: the consumer.
Citizen accountability is crucial if we are to establish effective legislation.
And to this end, my dear Karl Heinz, the eco-contribution has proven to be one of the most relevant tools, among other systems.

Electrical and electronic equipment producers must obviously take greater responsibility within the collection and recycling system, but citizens cannot be exempted from accountability for the waste they generate.
Our citizens must be actors, not spectators, of European environmental policy.
Madam President, Mr President, Commissioner, I would also like to echo the remark made just now by Mrs McGuinness regarding the essential link between food issues and agricultural issues.
The issue that concerns us is the rise in food prices, along with the consequences this may have on a number of populations around the world, as well as in Europe.
I would first like to try to share an observation.

The trend toward rising agricultural prices and agricultural commodity prices is, in my view, a long-term trend that will persist for three fundamental reasons: the demographic evolution of the world and the increasing

of the world's population. Second, the fact that we have returned, with climate change, to climatic hazards that pose problems, whether droughts or floods. And the third point, because the world is also developing,
With newly emerging countries, consumption levels are increasing.
Thus, the pressure on agricultural markets and agricultural raw materials is a major trend.
It is exacerbated by price volatility, and price volatility is a consequence of the financialization of the global economy.
And it is on this very issue that we must also focus, if we are to resolve the agricultural and food issues.

And to achieve this, what the Commissioner has said is important: there are two elements. Yes, there must be transparency regarding stockpiles, but there must also be a public policy on stockpiles in Europe and coordinated global stock management, particularly at the United Nations level.

Second point, financial markets and financial speculation must be regulated, particularly by ensuring that speculative volumes no longer have any connection whatsoever with the physical quantities traded on the markets.

We cannot accept that, on the issue of food, speculators come to speculate without regard for the consequences this may have, since we are dealing with the food of the world and humanity. I conclude by saying
This leads me to two major consequences: we need a strong European policy, and we reaffirm strategically Europe's commitment to being a global actor in food and agriculture.

Secondly, we need to change our development policy so that, on a global scale and with Europe as an active player, we reinvest in agriculture, since we will need all forms of agriculture to feed the world.
Madam President, Commissioner, as in 2008, agricultural commodity prices are rising on global markets.
We are only three short of the two thousand mark.
The decline in production in certain traditional exporting regions, due to climate change, the diversion of cereals toward biofuel production, and speculation
Financial actions have had inflationary effects and are responsible for this situation, as shown by reports from the IMF, French authorities, and also stated by the Commission.
The consequences of this new food crisis are severely affecting 1.2 billion people worldwide who live below one and a half dollars a day.
Since October, forty-four million additional people have joined those living below the poverty line.
Two days before the G20 summit, what is Europe waiting for to take similar measures to those adopted in the United States?
Since January 13, 2011, the U.S. administration has required the limitation and transparency of positions taken by financial funds on agricultural commodity markets.
This advancement is the first step toward countering the ambitions of speculators.
The impact is also dramatic for European farmers.
They can no longer feed their animals.
Hundreds of farms are bankrupt.
Five years ago, wheat was worth one hundred euros per tonne; today it is trading at nearly three hundred euros.
The situation has therefore completely changed.
The Commission demands that farmers adapt to market signals; it would do well today to follow its own advice.
Faced with this crisis, the European Union cannot wait until 2014 to find solutions.
We must confront the crisis, and I propose three concrete actions.
First, the creation of grain stocks in cereal-producing countries to make them available to livestock farmers at subsidized prices immediately.
Second, indexing meat prices paid to farmers to the fluctuations in livestock feed prices.
Thirdly, for 2011, a mechanism transferring funds from support for cereal production to animal production. Thank you.
Mr. President, are we ready in Europe to face a pandemic?
The answer is yes, but there is a caveat.
uppercase. The H1N1 flu episode of 2009 showed us that each member state reacted on its own behalf, without much coherence or even solidarity.

As the fictitious rapporteur on this file, I believe it was necessary to make these observations, but above all to go further, to be constructive and concrete for our citizens, who, we must acknowledge—and this is, in my view, the most regrettable aspect—have undoubtedly lost some trust in public health messages.

Ms. Rivasi's report, which will be voted on tomorrow, takes into account these fundamental priorities, including the systematic strengthening of cooperation among member states, and better coordination with the
European health institutions, a clarification of the roles of European health risk management structures, an assessment of vaccination and communication strategies implemented by member states.
It is therefore a good report; I congratulate the rapporteur. Naturally, it must not remain a dead letter.
It is the responsibility of our member states, which retain the major share of competence in this area, and thus it is a genuine responsibility to be upheld toward our citizens.

Mr. President, I would like, in turn, to express my profound emotion following the cowardly assassination on March 2nd of Mr. Bhatti, the Pakistani minister responsible for Christian minority affairs.
This terrorist assassination follows numerous others targeting courageous, tolerant Pakistanis who love human rights, human rights defenders, and women's rights defenders, I must say.

Once again, we urgently call on the Pakistani government to shed full light on this cowardly assassination and to effectively, truly effectively, protect those threatened by fanatical religious extremists.
I naturally think first and foremost, though not exclusively, of Madame Sherry Raymond.
I would also like, once again, vigorously call upon all competent institutions of the European Union to include in the negotiations aimed at
prepare future cooperation agreements, in particular the cooperation agreement between the EU and Pakistan, including a clause on respect for religious freedom and human rights. Thank you.
Mr. President, for the record, due to the crisis and the bursting of the housing and credit bubbles, the Irish State was forced to intervene to support the banks.
Ireland's public deficit and the cumulative budget balance of central, local, and particularly social security administrations were estimated at 32% of GDP in 2010.

If the Irish government, which finds it difficult to refinance itself on financial markets, wishes to obtain loans from the mechanism and the European Financial Stability Facility, it will have to meet obligations regarding social and fiscal reforms, even though these areas fall under the principle of subsidiarity.
In our view, this is not contradictory.
As a physician, I would say that in cases of fever, it is pointless to prescribe antipyretics blindly without seeking to treat the underlying cause.
In the event that these funds are allocated, it is up to that State to restore the soundness of its finances in order to eliminate its excessive deficit and rectify its prior shortcomings.
This austerity plan should increase GDP by ten points over the next four years.

Just as a private individual, a bank will not interfere in their personal life or private affairs but will condition a personal loan on a guarantee of solvency, so too can the European Union lend to Ireland on the basis of consolidated solvency.
Without interfering in its private management, the bank will encourage the individual to negotiate, for example, a salary increase or to borrow elsewhere.

Thus, respecting subsidiarity, the European Union, based on a memorandum established with the IMF, considers that this solvency requires reducing the minimum wage and adjusting pensions, without exempting itself from fiscal measures.
It is up to Ireland to decide whether or not to implement these measures.

It is not a gateway to European Union interference in areas of subsidiarity that would, for example, dangerously impose a European minimum wage level, but rather a protection of the stability of the eurozone through a process of mutual guarantee.

Of course, we should assist Ireland, as we should other countries facing difficulties, as this also protects us; but not at any price, particularly not at the cost of allowing a state unable to repay its debts in the long term, thereby weakening both its own citizens and those of the entire European Union.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues, the eruption of the Icelandic volcano last April, as many of us have just said, provided a painful illustration of the fragmentation and inefficiency of the European sky.
and each of us here in this chamber has been directly affected.
and five million of our fellow citizens found themselves stranded, with no genuine alternative means of transportation available.
One year after this exceptional event, new European measures are still awaited.

A first crisis exercise, as you mentioned, Mr. Commissioner, related to volcanic ash is scheduled for the 13th and 14th of April next year, under the coordination of Eurocontrol, ICAO, and IATA; however, it is essential that the AESA assume real leadership in coordinating safety measures at the European level.
It is certain that the information provided by the London and Toulouse VACs must be refined using more modern technological means.

These data, as seen last April, may nevertheless be contradictory and could lead each Member State to make decisions regarding the closure of its airspace less motivated by information than by emotion.
In this context, the role of the AESA is essential in providing a harmonized interpretation of these data, as well as ensuring the centralization and effective dissemination of information.
Beyond informing the aircraft, the AESA must establish a data collection mechanism by closely involving the Member States, airlines, and their pilots.
This centralization of information and coordination of all involved parties will prevent the chaos and inconsistencies we witnessed last year.
I therefore expect the Commission, in collaboration with the AESA, to swiftly present concrete proposals that will be fully integrated into the implementation of the European Sky.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, this text is important.
It is not a revolution; one cannot claim that everything was bad before and perfect now.
Nevertheless, it does present a number of significant advances at this provisional stage of our work.

In my opinion, the main issues concern better regulation of distance selling, and in particular online sales, which is the subject of chapter three, as consumer rights in this area need to be strengthened.
E-commerce is highly beneficial: it broadens consumer choice, enables consumers to obtain the lowest prices, and also allows small and medium-sized enterprises and craftsmen to access new markets.

To make progress in this area, it was necessary to proceed in a truly European manner, meaning that, on a few specific points, a complete harmonization of everyone's rights and obligations had to be established.

Very concretely, it was essential that an Italian customer purchasing from a Belgian website or a German website should be aware that, from now on, they would enjoy the same rights and the same protection.
This is precisely what this directive will make possible.
This means that its withdrawal period will be extended to fourteen days throughout the Union.
This means he will immediately know the total amount he has to pay, without fearing an unpleasant surprise afterward.
This means that, thanks to the double click, it will properly verify its order.
Of course, all of this will disrupt certain habits.
In many states, there are subtleties in national law that everyone considers fundamental, even though such elements are sometimes entirely absent in neighboring countries.
But living together in Europe means learning to slightly modify one's habits in the service of the common and general interest.
Let us be clear: this text will still need to be improved, and we will have to do so in the coming weeks in collaboration with the Council and the Commission.
I appreciate the openings that have just been made in this regard.
But we have already made substantial progress thanks to our rapporteur, Andrea Schwab, whom I warmly thank.
But the real outcome of this work is that the amendments will be voted on tomorrow by all political groups.
Consumer confidence in the internal market of the Union is very fragile.
This political unanimity was necessary to preserve it and to hope to strengthen it.
At point seven, after the first paragraph, a first dash ensures respect for human rights and fundamental freedoms, in particular the
freedom of speech and assembly, and the right to peacefully demonstrate, as well as the duty to listen to and address the legitimate demands of demonstrators and ensure their safety.
Madam President, dear colleagues, Commissioner, the Open Skies agreement between Europe and the United States is a step forward, but it is only a first step—one that places Europe at a crossroads.
The Transport Committee and the Committee itself have emphasized this point.
Either we accept an agreement that disadvantages European businesses, or we suspend the agreement and risk losing the benefits gained since 2008.
Let me therefore draw a parallel with private and professional pilot licenses, a text that will be discussed before summer, as the pilots I belong to are facing a dilemma.
The European Union Aviation Safety Agency, aiming to harmonize licenses across the European territory, will do so at the expense of approximately eighty to ninety general aviation pilots.
directly involved, some holding only a single U.S. license, and to the detriment—this is crucial—of businesses and jobs related to this activity.
I would like to draw your attention to this project, which lacks a security basis.
There are thirty-one times fewer accidents among holders of American licenses.
