If written consultations are not overrepresented due solely to availability in English, for example, they fail to achieve their objective of consulting European citizens.
Thus, the result provides a misleading indication and should therefore not be used by the European Commission.
If we wish to maintain written consultations as a foundation for the European Commission's legislative proposals, we must ensure a democratic, transparent, and fair process for all citizens.
Thus, if the European Commission chooses written consultations, it must assume its responsibilities and provide the necessary resources for multilingualism and the translation of questionnaires.
To conclude, I would like to add that multilingualism is not the only obstacle

It would also be appropriate to include in the analysis the excessively short timeframes for submitting contributions, as well as the limited efforts made by the Commission to communicate about these consultations, which remain unfortunately accessible mainly to insiders. Thank you.
The proposed regulation aims to grant the European Commission enhanced powers to oversee national budgets and economic policies.
The rules will be stricter, and failing states will be forced by Brussels technocrats to implement budget cuts and structural reforms in order to restore their financial balances.
This enhanced supervision is called guardianship.
States will be punished, fined, disciplined, one might say, financial sanctions will be strengthened and more automatic.
Even though some amendments aim to prevent fiscal austerity alone by including growth measures, your proposals are merely intended to make the pill easier to swallow.
Growth, as you know as well as I do, cannot be decreed.
Without consulting the peoples and with the complicity of Brussels, we are definitively losing our budgetary sovereignty and our freedom to choose our policies.

And when, little by little, according to the technique of the Chinese thief, the bureaucratic, technocratic, and totalitarian European state has finally been established, we will have lost our freedom as a people in its entirety.
Mr. President, while not diminishing the importance of the economic choices we must make, you will allow me to emphasize a point of a more political nature within the context of this debate.
In recent weeks, we have again witnessed evidence of the double standards adopted by certain major European leaders—or those who consider themselves as such.
While everyone continues to advocate for economic, budgetary, and fiscal policies finally worthy of the name, and while this requirement itself appears as a condition
a sine qua non for the survival of the eurozone, here we have Mr. Croix Malin, once comfortably settled at home, denouncing the balanced recommendations emanating from
the Commission seeking to free itself from certain commitments, or, in the worst case, mobilizing public opinion against these Brussels edicts, as they are called.
This irresponsible attitude might be laughable if we were not in such a tragic situation.
I say this quite plainly: our debates on stability, the necessary整顿 of public finances, or the efforts required in terms of growth are indeed destined to
failure if past shortcomings in public debt and deficit management do not come to an end and if member states' governments continue along their current path of mismanagement.
We are currently playing with fire.
European discipline must apply to all, without exception—even to the Grand Duchy of Luxembourg.
This requires a minimum of courage, and our public opinions are ready for it, far more than some of their leaders care to believe.
And one final word to Ms. Goulard, otherwise I would have to show her the bank card; I would say, to conclude, Ms. Goulard, in redemption, there is repurchase.

Mr. President, I would first like to commend the determination of the European Commission, and in particular your own, Mr. Commissioner, in tirelessly placing the revitalization and deepening of the internal market at the top of the European agenda.

As you reminded us, Mr. Commissioner, it has already been over two years since both the Commission and the European Parliament have been working to identify the missing links in the internal market, to pinpoint the issues and sectors where greater internal market integration is needed—more internal market integration for greater growth, greater competitiveness, and more jobs.

The European Council, for its part, also eventually emphasized, albeit later, the importance it attached to the swift and effective implementation of the twelve priority proposals for the internal market, the twelve levers for growth.
Rather than continually striving to invent new growth instruments, we stand to gain by working with the toolkit already at our disposal.

We therefore take the opportunity provided by the resolution to be voted on tomorrow by our Assembly to send a strong political message to the Member States demanding greater speed and efficiency in transposing existing directives.
adopted more mobilization and commitment in the newly opened projects; I will mention only one, that of enhancing workers' mobility—greater ease of movement, greater flow of circulation.
We will welcome with great interest, Mr. Commissioner, the second phase of the action for the single market that you will present after the summer.
We will demonstrate both political will and the ability to put pressure on the member states.
We recently experienced the scandal involving the ombudsman.
We now have the scandal involving breast implants, which unfortunately highlights, as you mentioned, Mr. Commissioner, the shortcomings in European public health safety.
This scandal involving breast implants, these fourteen years of fraud from 1996 to 2010, unpunished,
There are five hundred prostheses in use worldwide, these eight implant removals, and forty-eight confirmed cases of cancer.
There are finally three complaints filed. These figures are alarming, shocking.

How, despite the warnings issued by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), Mr. Commissioner, and the numerous complaints and convictions related to implant failures filed in Great Britain and France in particular, how were national and European agencies able to overlook such a blatant fraud?
This tragedy immediately led us to consider improving the European system for controlling medical devices, as you have just explained to us.
Mr. Commissioner, today we must build a new system capable of preventing scandal—but it is a new system that must be built.
It has been announced for autumn, as you mentioned, this new legislation.
As co-author of this resolution, I am naturally satisfied with it.
However, Mr. Commissioner, you appear to be taking into account a large part of our concerns as expressed in this resolution.
So why make an unacceptable exception for so many parliamentarians present here?
Why would one not want the creation of a marketing authorization for the highest-risk class three medical devices?
Commissioner Dali, could you please explain your position?
Mr. President, currently we are experiencing a situation with a production increase of more than two and a half. At the same time, industrialists are lowering prices.
In Italy, Germany, England, and across all European countries, prices are declining.
This clearly means that prices are linked to the volume of production, and we must therefore organize both production volumes and prices simultaneously.
At the same time, today, producers are suffering from an increase of over forty-four percent in vegetable protein prices and thirty percent in cereal prices.
The Parliament has already voted in favor of the necessity here to ensure that the added value is shared between producers, so that farmers are not victims of companies and large retailers.

I therefore call on the Commission today, as well as on the Council, to reconsider the challenge to production control; at the same time, I make it very clear that Member States must clean up their own houses, because there are countries that have respected quota rules and others that have not.
And it is precisely this that led to these quotas being challenged.
There is thus a dual responsibility: the Commission and the Member States.
Commissioner, dear colleagues, a unique European railway space—now that is a commendable policy of European integration.
Here is a fine policy in which European integration lies at the heart of the daily concerns of European women and men.

Too often criticized for being ineffective, Europe finally, through this directive, demonstrates the path toward a major sustainable transport policy and opens the way for a large-scale railway investment policy aimed at employment.

I welcome the key measures taken in organizing the railway network—the establishment of national regulators in each Member State, and the creation of a five-year multiannual investment contract, thereby specifying funding commitments.

railway infrastructure, but I also welcome the establishment of transparency in financial flows between managers and operators within integrated companies. The reminder of non-discriminatory access to certain

But allow me to raise a requirement that we cease considering, particularly from the European Commission, the liberalization of the railway sector as the sole strategic objective for developing a unified railway space, and instead adopt a European integrated investment strategy conducive to employment across Europe.

Here, at the moment when European citizens, as a whole,
Thank you, Mr. President. At this point, much has already been said.
Mr. Commissioner, for my part, I would like to thank you most warmly.
Thanks to your persistence, your procedural tenacity, you enable us to demonstrate the importance of the European Parliament as a co-legislator.
Thank you, Mr. Commissioner, for having mobilized hundreds of thousands of citizens to take to the streets and for having secured two million eight hundred thousand signatures on petitions.
They realized that democracy is played out here, that we, the elected European representatives, are the representatives of the people, their representatives.
Thank you once again to the millions of internet users who followed the committee debates and voting, and who continue to follow us today.
I greet them. It is a breath of fresh air within our institutions, too often perceived as technocratic.
Mr. Commissioner, but in a democracy, there is also another principle.

Tomorrow, we will take our responsibility, and I would like, tomorrow, to also thank you for assuming your responsibility, your failure, the failure of your work method.
I am speaking on behalf of Maurice Ponga, the opinion rapporteur of the Development Committee on Mr. Gallagher's report.
It seemed important to emphasize that the European Union, when seeking to sanction a country engaged in unsustainable fishing practices, must take into account its level of development.

Indeed, in the name of the principle of policy coherence with development objectives, I consider it essential that measures adopted in the field of fisheries do not undermine the development plans and strategies established by the European Union in these countries.
Mr. Gallagher's draft report takes up this important point for our committee on development by stipulating that measures must not be unjustified, nor even discriminatory.

I therefore urge the European Commission, when adopting such measures, to respect the principle of policy coherence with development objectives and to duly take into account the level of development and vulnerability of countries. Thank you.
This third reform of the common fisheries policy must not once again be carried out at the expense of fishermen's interests.

No, not all fishers are these plunderers of the sea, the sole culprits behind the poor health of fish stocks, as environmental organizations and the European Commission claim when encompassing all categories of fishing.

It is clearly necessary to preserve fish stocks, but we must also enable our fishermen to earn a living from their activity and safeguard the economic fabric, vitality, and cultural identity of our coastal regions.
Indeed, the European executive's main measures once again point toward reducing the number of fishermen in order to achieve the mythical maximum sustainable yield.
I recall that the French fleet has decreased by more than fifty since 1980.

The Commission notably proposes the introduction of individual transferable quotas, seen as indicators of industrialization and sectoral consolidation, as well as a complete ban on discharges, which professionals consider unrealistic.
This reform proposal likely suits the industrial fleet, but as it stands would mean the programmed demise of what remains of local and artisanal fishing.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, I would like to congratulate the PPE rapporteur, our colleague Karins, for this difficult report, given the sensitive nature of bilateral energy negotiations between European countries and third states.
The agreement reached in trilogue will enable the swift implementation of the Commission's proposed decision, and, given a commitment to pragmatism, one can only welcome this development.
Europe's energy transition is progressing too slowly, certainly, but it is progressing.
As a major energy importer, the European Union is structurally dependent on certain supplier countries.

In order to foster a more European reflection on our energy strategy, it became necessary to disseminate energy-related agreements more widely to the European Commission, while respecting the principle of subsidiarity.
Establishing a mechanism for information on intergovernmental agreements will therefore enhance transparency in the energy market and enable anticipation of energy supply issues.
I welcome the fact that the present decision does not create any obligation regarding agreements between commercial companies.

In times of crisis and given the stakes regarding competition and competitiveness, we had a duty to protect the interests of our businesses and the principles governing the protection of private contracts.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, I would like to thank our two rapporteurs for the work they have carried out on this file.

It was necessary because we were starting from a very weak position based on the Commission's initial proposal, which made no specific mention of the ultra-peripheral regions directly affected by competition from Latin American countries, and which provided no automatic trigger

of the stabilization mechanism, no possibility offered to our Assembly to request the opening of a safeguard investigation, no consideration given to social and environmental standards as potentially constituting an unfair competitive advantage.
This is why it is extremely important that tomorrow's vote endorse the progress achieved on these issues in the Committee on International Trade.
Nevertheless, this is not sufficient, as we know that the triggering thresholds for these two regulations are so high that they render these mechanisms practically inapplicable.
Moreover, the Commission must allocate an additional financial envelope to support the competitiveness of Community banana production.
Let us keep in mind that many jobs are at stake in these European regions, which already have record unemployment rates.
Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, I would first like to thank Charles Goerens for his excellent report.

We all know here that the European Union provides more than half of the world's total public development assistance, and yet, as the 2015 deadline for the MDGs approaches, even though some targets appear to
Affected, we must ask ourselves how to improve our assistance, making it more consistent with our trade policy, our agricultural policy, and our foreign policy.
The future of European development policy must be based on precise, measurable commitments, with a short- and medium-term timeline.

The economic crisis, climate change, the rising power of emerging countries, and land grabbing compel us to establish new priorities to ensure food security, access to energy resources, and access to healthcare.

Certainly, a European consensus exists, but we must rethink European and international environmental and social institutional systems, drawing inspiration from bodies that function effectively in decision-making, such as the WTO, for example.
Finally and above all, we must explain to our fellow citizens that the development of third countries is not merely a matter of charity and solidarity, but also a source of development for us.
One need only look at the markets opening up for Europeans in emerging countries such as China, Brazil, and India.
2015 will also provide an opportunity to better account for the geostrategic changes of recent years, as today's emerging countries were only recently developing nations.
Now that these countries have become indispensable economic powers, they must also contribute to international aid.
The European Union must be the driving force behind responsible development aid at the European and international levels.

The year 2015 will be more than a deadline; it will be a unique opportunity to rethink development aid, no longer as mere financial assistance, but as support for responsible development initiatives, both socially and environmentally.

I therefore join my colleagues from the other groups, as well as those from the Economic and Social Committee and the Committee of the Regions, in calling for 2015 to be designated as the European Year of Development and, above all, of responsible development.
Mr. President, in my view, the agenda for change proposed by the Commission raises numerous questions, the main one being the practical implementation of the principle of differentiation.

The Commission wishes that, starting from 2014, the selection of countries in which the European Union will channel its development aid be based mechanically on the single criterion of GDP per capita.
Or, as Ms. Ashton has reminded us, these issues are not merely accounting matters, but warrant a genuine political debate about defining our priorities in international cooperation.
Moreover, in my view, this differentiated approach is not consistent with the spirit of the Cotonou Agreement.

I am convinced that ACP countries that have achieved positive economic performance should be encouraged in their transition toward more sustainable growth. These countries should not be unfairly penalized for having successfully ensured growth through discipline, sound governance, and prudent economic policies.
Madam President, Commissioner, let us salute the initiative taken by Jacques Delors twenty years ago, at the very moment when the Berlin Wall fell.

Despite this, despite all the progress we have observed since the beginning of our debate, it must be acknowledged that the Single Act is today synonymous with injustice, inequality, and economic liberalism.
I would therefore like to hear you, Mr. Barnier, Commissioner, to explain how we will jointly build a fairer Europe, a facilitating Europe, a protective Europe.

There are a number of avenues, and I would like to hear your thoughts on these, which lie at the heart of the economic and social crisis as unfortunately, we know it: tax harmonization, public services,
champion of the minimum wage in Europe, establishment of enhanced cooperation initiatives that generate jobs, facilitation of cross-border cooperation, and development of a policy for fair exchange.
Here are the challenges, I believe, that lie before us twenty years later.
I rely on you, Mr. Commissioner, to jointly advance this excellent policy of European construction.

Mr. President, as a member of the Committee on the Internal Market and Consumer Protection, I have closely followed my colleague Regina Bastos's report on the twenty main concerns of citizens and businesses.
and we have discussed this report, moreover in connection with our debate on the twenty years of the internal market.
It has already been twenty years since the free movement of goods, people, services, and capital has been supposed to be fully operational within our space of life, exchange, and single market.

I have been particularly struck by the fact that among the foremost concerns expressed by our European fellow citizens is the issue of professional qualifications, and that we must therefore be genuinely proactive and highly effective.

This is what our fellow citizens expect in order to truly establish, in the very near future, procedures for recognizing their qualifications, skills, and professional credentials that are genuinely faster, simpler, more effective, and less costly.
It is a genuine issue.

As the fictitious rapporteur for the PPE group on this issue—modernizing these procedures—I am actively working with the other members of the IMCO committee to address this major challenge to mobility.
Greater mobility, for a greater Europe—this is what our fellow citizens are asking for. Thank you.
Mr. President, this year the Petitions Committee has acquired the right to be automatically associated with the hearings on European Citizens' Initiatives.
For me, it is a clear signal of the year 2011 for this Commission.
Citizens already had a body to approach in the event of a violation of their rights, but they will now also have such a recourse when they have proposals to improve European legislation.
I hope that the Committee on Petitions will thus increasingly be seen as the preferred point of contact for citizens within the European Parliament.
This issue of consideration is also present in the findings of the Committee on Petitions' positions.
Too often, we are approached by citizens facing legal entanglements about which we would like to take a stance. But while the issues are European, then
that solutions should be European, we remain powerless, as subsidiarity still does not align with European solutions due to a lack of national will.
I will take the difficult example of cross-border divorces. The issues are both national and European when custody or visitation rights extend beyond national borders, in this case those of Germany.
Despite the obvious difficulties faced by many parents whose children are born from binational unions, we remain powerless to act while still in Parliament.

My proposal that all European citizens should have the right to a national ombudsman, particularly one originating from Germany, which submits an enormous number of petitions in the style of France's Defender of Rights, has had no effect.
Yet, in the case of transnational divorces, this remains a solution that reconciles a problematic European dimension with a national resolution.
In many cases, European law appears powerless to meet citizens' expectations.
